trait.name: Hallucinogenic Gas
entries: After the green dragon exhales its Poison Breath, wisps of poisonous gas laced with the alchemical taint that permeates the dragon’s body linger around it. While the dragon’s Poison Breath is unavailable, each creature that starts its turn within 20 feet of the dragon must succeed on a DC 18 WIS save or be terrified, as the gas causes the creature to experience frightful hallucinations. A terrified creature is frightened, and its speed is reduced to 0.

trait.name: Legendary Resistance (3/Day)
entries: If the dragon fails a save, it can choose to succeed instead.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The dragon uses its Frightful Presence. It then makes one Bite attack and two Claw attacks.

action.name: Bite
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +11 to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. Hit: 17 (2d10 + 6) piercing damage plus 7 (2d6) poison damage.

action.name: Claw
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +11 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 13 (2d6 + 6) slashing damage.

action.name: Tail
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +11 to hit, reach 15 ft., one target. Hit: 15 (2d8 + 6) bludgeoning damage.

action.name: Frightful Presence
entries: Each creature of the dragon’s choice that is within 120 feet of the dragon and aware of it must succeed on a DC 16 WIS save or be frightened for 1 minute. A creature can repeat the save at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success. If a creature’s save is successful or the effect ends for it, the creature is immune to the dragon’s Frightful Presence for the next 24 hours.

action.name: Poison Breath (Recharge 5–6)
entries: The dragon exhales poisonous gas in a 60-foot cone. Each creature in that area must make a DC 18 CON save, taking 56 (16d6) poison damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

legendary.name: Text
entries: The dragon can take 3 legendary actions, choosing from the options below. Only one legendary action can be used at a time and only at the end of another creature’s turn. The dragon regains spent legendary actions at the start of its turn.

legendary.name: Detect
entries: The dragon automatically detects every creature and trap within 30 feet of it, pinpointing the current location of each.

legendary.name: Tail Attack
entries: The dragon makes a Tail attack.

legendary.name: Elemental Roar (Costs 2 Actions)
entries: The dragon roars at up to two creatures of its choice within 120 feet of it. Each target must succeed on a DC 16 CHA save or be vulnerable to poison damage until the end of its next turn. A target resistant to poison damage isn’t resistant to it for the duration. A target immune to poison damage is still immune to it, even if the target fails the save. Wing Attack (Costs 2 Actions). The dragon beats its wings. Each creature within 10 feet of the dragon must succeed on a DC 19 DEX save or take 13 (2d6 + 6) bludgeoning damage and be knocked prone. The dragon can then fly up to half its flying speed without provoking opportunity attacks.

A creature can repeat the save at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success. If a creature’s save is Dragon, Red A mighty dragon with bright red scales swoops down from the mountain, setting the countryside ablaze with its fiery breath.

action.name: Avaricious and Territorial
entries: All dragons hoard treasure, but the greed of the red dragon reigns supreme. They prefer quantity over quality, prominently displaying stolen gold and the skulls of their foes. In twisting tunnels in the fiery belly of the earth, they protect their fabulous wealth with cunning traps and terrible guardians. Red dragons brook no incursions into their territory, which can stretch for miles around their lairs.

action.name: Dominating Pride
entries: Red dragons truly believe they are the mightiest of all dragons and superior to all other creatures. They are quick to boast of their own greatness and happy to demonstrate their supremacy by fang or fire. This pride makes them tyrannical, and many red dragons delight in forcing tribute or service from lesser creatures.

action.name: Incendiary Rage
entries: The magical core of fire at the heart of every red dragon both generates their incinerating breath and makes them quick to anger. They take offense at even slight provocation, flying into devastating rages that last until their fury is vented.

action.name: Maturing
entries: Red wyrmlings hatch on remote mountain cliffs and peaks far from the lairs of other dragons. Even at that age, they are quick to carve out a territory, attempting to subjugate any who dare enter their territory. Many wyrmlings don’t survive past this age, their aggression often exceeding their ability to win the fights they start. By the time the dragon is young, it bears several scars, each a story of hard-won victory and every inch of territory maintained. During this time, the dragon starts to hoard treasures it gathers from frightened or slain travelers, preferring gold and all things that glitter. As an adult, the red dragon has claimed a lair and already accumulated significant wealth, aggressively protecting it and the surrounding territory. At this age, some red dragons make pacts with nearby humanoids, offering—and usually providing—protection in exchange for regular tributes of gold or meat. Ancient red dragons typically have several groups or settlements of humanoids in their service and spend their days gloating over past glories atop a mountain of gold. A RED DRAGON’S LAIR A red dragon favors high mountains, claiming snowy peaks, fuming volcanoes, or sprawling ancient ruins. A red dragon’s immense strength, agile flight, and comfort in blistering temperatures make its lair difficult to traverse for most other creatures. From its skyward vantage point, a red dragon peers down at what it considers a world of lesser creatures. In the most extreme lairs, perched above the churning interior of active volcanoes, red dragons retain azers, fire elementals, fire giants, salamanders, and devils or demons to serve their whims. Sprawling dwarven tunnels, forgotten giant foundries, or lakes of boiling stone make the perfect respite for a red dragon’s nest. LAIR ACTIONS On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), the dragon can take a lair action to cause one of the following effects; the dragon can’t use the same effect two rounds in a row:
• Collapse. The ceiling on a point the dragon can see

within 120 feet of it collapses, covering a 20-foot square area on the ground directly below that point. Each creature in that area must succeed on a DC 15 DEX save or be knocked prone and buried under the rubble. A buried creature is restrained and unable to breathe or stand up. A creature, including the buried creature, can take its action to free the buried creature by succeeding on a DC 15 STR check while within reach of the buried creature.
• Swirling Ash. Searing ash blasts a point the dragon can

see within 120 feet of it. Each creature within 30 feet of that point must make a DC 15 CON save. On a failure, a creature takes 10 (3d6) fire damage and is blinded until the end of its next turn. On a success, a creature takes half the damage and isn’t blinded. The ash ignites flammable objects within 30 feet of that point that aren’t being worn or carried.
• Conflagrate. One creature the dragon can see within

120 feet of it becomes tethered to the Elemental Plane of Fire. The target must succeed on a DC 15 CHA save or be burned for 1 minute. While burned, the target takes 7 (2d6) fire damage at the start of each of its turns. The effect ends early if the target takes cold damage, is submerged in water, or the burn is removed with a greater restoration spell or similar magic. REGIONAL EFFECTS The region containing a red dragon’s lair is suffused with fiery chaos, which creates one or more of the following effects:
• Acid Rain. Within 10
miles of the red dragon’s lair, precipitation is oily and stinks of sulfur. Snow in the area may be gray or black as it falls, appearing like ash, though its temperature is unchanged.
• Incited Rage. Creatures within 3 miles of the red

dragon’s lair are quick to anger and slow to forgive. Such creatures have disadvantage on CHA checks, except CHA (Intimidation), and Beasts in the area that aren’t domesticated are more aggressive than normal.
• Unnatural Heat. Within 1 mile of the red dragon’s

lair, temperatures are unnaturally high. Vegetation in the area withers and eventually crumbles to dust. Creatures in the area require twice as much water as normal each day to survive, and each creature in the area must succeed on a DC 10 CON save every hour it remains in the area or suffer one level of exhaustion. Creatures that are resistant or immune to fire damage are unaffected by this regional effect. If the dragon dies, these effects fade in 1d10 days.

trait.name: Pounce
entries: If the dragon moves at least 15 feet straight toward a creature and then hits it with a Claw attack on the same turn, that target must succeed on a DC 14 STR save or be knocked prone. If the target is prone, the dragon can make one Claw attack against it as a bonus action.

legendary.name: Wing Attack (Costs 2 Actions)
entries: The dragon beats its wings. Each creature within 15 feet of the dragon must succeed on a DC 25 DEX save or take 17 (2d6 + 10) bludgeoning damage and be knocked prone. The dragon can then fly up to half its flying speed without provoking opportunity attacks. Dragon, Silver This dragon shimmers like polished silver as it moves. Large scales like layered breastplates run the length of its spine, and a pair of horns curves forward from its skull. A frosty chill hangs in the air around it.

legendary.name: Kindhearted Watchers
entries: Looking down from their mountain perches, silver dragons see it as their duty to befriend and protect other sentient beings in their territory. Aloof and watchful, they watch over ages as civilizations develop, occasionally adopting disguises to provide guiding nudges. Elders warn young silver dragons to avoid direct entanglement with humanoid affairs. Some can’t help themselves, and inadvertently become weapons of war, or give rise to magical, royal bloodlines.

legendary.name: For the Greater Good
entries: Silver dragons believe in promoting the greatest good for the greatest number of beings, especially dragons. This stance can sometimes lead the dragon to commit or condone acts many would consider less than good. However, the dragon’s actions operate across centuries and millennia, and it knows the steps it takes in the short term will benefit many more in the long term. Silver dragons focus on the larger picture and life as a whole, combating great evils and calamities on cosmic time scales .

legendary.name: Historical Hoard
entries: Silver dragon hoards are museums of their favored cultures. Their lairs are filled with artifacts from these civilizations—coins, fine arts, tapestries, manuscripts, epic poems, and historical treatises. The hoard might also contain the intact sarcophagus of the first ruler of the first nation the silver dragon watched over, the armor of an adopted civilization’s first great warrior, or the favored trinket of the first creature the dragon befriended.

legendary.name: Maturing
entries: Silver dragons spend the first few years of their lives with their parents, learning about various creatures and the draconic role in maintaining the betterment of life across millennia. By the time the dragon is young, it has developed a fondness for a particular creature, people, or culture and seeks to study its new obsession from a distance, often in the guise of a beast of burden or traveler. By the time the dragon is an adult, it has carved out its own small lair where it maintains a few souvenirs from beloved places or people and records their achievements or failings. With vast patience, ancient silver dragons often spend decades resting in their lairs, coming out every few years to check on the progress of nearby lands and encouraging small changes to foster a better world. A SILVER DRAGON’S LAIR Silver dragon lairs are always found at high altitudes. Caverns amid snowy peaks are common. However, the most prized locales for a silver dragon’s lair are remnants of the civilizations they watched grow and fall. A mountain fortress overlooking a secluded pass, an abandoned wizard’s tower, or even the former fortress of a cloud giant clan makes an ideal lair for a silver dragon. LAIR ACTIONS On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), the dragon can take a lair action to cause one of the following effects; the dragon can’t use the same effect two rounds in a row:
• Enthralling Reflection. Icy surfaces within the lair

shine mirror bright. Each creature within 20 feet of a point on a frozen surface the dragon can see within 120 feet of it must succeed on a DC 15 CHA save or be charmed until initiative count 20 of the following round. While charmed, a creature is incapacitated, and if it is more than 5 feet from the surface, it must move on its turn toward the surface, trying to get within 5 feet. It doesn’t avoid opportunity attacks or hazardous terrain. If a charmed creature takes damage, the charmed effect ends for that creature.
• Flash Freeze. Up to two targets the dragon can see

within 120 feet of it must succeed on a DC 15 CON save or be restrained for 1 minute as its body begins to freeze. At the end of each of its turns, it can repeat the save. On a success, the effect ends for the creature. If the creature fails this save three times, it is petrified in ice until the condition is removed.
• Blizzard. The dragon creates a 20-foot-radius sphere of

driving snow centered on a point it can see within 120 feet. The sphere spreads around corners, and its area is heavily obscured. Each creature that enters the area for the first time on a turn or starts its turn there must succeed a DC 15 CON save or take 7 (2d6) cold damage and its speed is halved until the end of its turn. The sphere lasts until the dragon uses this lair action again or until the dragon dies. REGIONAL EFFECTS The region containing a silver dragon’s lair is warped by its magical presence, which creates one or more of the following effects:
• Welcoming Chill. Humanoids within 10 miles of the

dragon’s lair remain comfortable in temperatures as low as −50 degrees Fahrenheit without any additional protection and can tolerate temperatures as low as −100 degrees Fahrenheit while wearing heavy clothes. The dragon can end this effect at will for any Humanoid it knows is within 10 miles of its lair.
• Ice Surveillance. Within 3 miles of the dragon’s lair,

the dragon can choose to see and hear through frozen ponds and puddles, icicles on buildings, columns of ice, ice sculptures, and similar areas of frozen water, and the dragon can speak through such ice while doing so.
• Disorienting Snow. Snow flurries dance through the

area within 1 mile of the dragon’s lair. A Humanoid hostile to the dragon and in this area must succeed on a DC 13 WIS save for every 10 minutes it travels through the area, becoming lost on a failed save. A lost Humanoid returns to the spot where it entered the area within 1 mile of the dragon’s lair, regardless of how far or for how long it traveled. If the dragon dies, these effects fade in 1d10 days.

entries: The dragon magically transforms into a 14 4 DD Beast or Humanoid that has a challenge rating no higher than its own, or back into its true form, which is Dragon. In a new form, the dragon retains its HP , ability to speak, proficiencies, and INT, WIS, and CHA scores, as well as this bonus action. Its statistics and capabilities are otherwise replaced by those of the new form, except any class features or legendary actions of that form.

entries: The dragon beats its wings. Each creature within 10 feet of the dragon must succeed on a DC 21 Dexterity save or take 15 (2d6 + 8) bludgeoning damage and be knocked prone. The dragon can then fly up to half its flying speed without provoking opportunity attacks.

trait.name: Freezing Fog
entries: As the young silver dragon, except it affects creatures within 30 feet of the dragon, and the save DC is 23.

trait.name: Legendary Resistance (3/Day)
entries: If the dragon fails a save, it can choose to succeed instead.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The dragon uses its Frightful Presence. It then makes one Bite attack and two Claw attacks. It can replace its Bite attack with a use of Paralyzing Breath, if available.

action.name: Claw

action.name: Tail

action.name: Frightful Presence

A creature can repeat the save at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success. If a creature’s save is successful or the effect ends for it, the creature is immune to the dragon’s Frightful Presence for the next 24 hours.

action.name: Cold Breath (Recharge 5–6)
entries: The dragon exhales an icy blast in a 90-foot cone. Each creature in that area must make a DC 23 CON save, taking 99 (22d8) cold damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

action.name: Paralyzing Breath (Recharge 6)
entries: As the silver dragon wyrmling, except it is a 90-foot cone, and the save DC is 23.

bonus.name: Change Shape
entries: The dragon magically transforms into a Beast or Humanoid that has a challenge rating no higher than its own, or back into its true form, which is Dragon. Any equipment it is wearing or carrying transforms with it or is borne by the new form (the dragon’s choice). It reverts to its true form if it dies. In a new form, the dragon retains its Legendary Resistance trait and its HP , ability to speak, proficiencies, and INT, WIS, and CHA scores, as well as this bonus action. Its statistics and capabilities are otherwise replaced by those of the new form, except any class features or legendary actions of that form.

The laws of reality repel the creatures back to their original positions as reality resets. The targets aren’t actually moved, but reality’s reset forces each target to make a DC 15 STR save. On a failure, the creature takes 16 (3d10) force damage and is knocked prone. On a success, the creature takes half the damage and isn’t knocked prone.
• Zero Gravity. Gravity stops working in a 50-foot radius,

100-foot-high cylinder centered on a point the dragon can see within 120 feet of it. Each creature in that cylinder drifts 10 feet away from the ground and must succeed on a DC 15 STR save or be restrained. A flying creature automatically succeeds on the save, but its speed is halved unless it can hover or flies magically. This effect lasts until initiative count 20 on the next round. REGIONAL EFFECTS The region containing a void dragon’s lair is warped by the dragon’s magic, which creates one or more of the following effects:
• Dark of the Void. Nonmagical illumination,
including sunlight, can’t create bright light within 6 miles of the lair.
• Torn Veil. Within 3 miles of the lair, disembodied voices
whisper in the night. Celestials, Fey, and Fiends with a challenge rating of 2 or lower can choose to slip into the Material Plane from their native planes or vice versa.
• Uncovered Knowledge. Secrets have a way of

coming to light within 6 miles of the lair. Clues are inadvertently discovered, slips of the tongue hint at a hidden truth, and creatures become morbidly curious for forbidden knowledge. If the dragon dies, these effects fade in 1d10 days. Dragon, Void A dragon seemingly formed of the night sky has bright white stars for eyes. Lesser stars twinkle in the firmament of the dragon’s body.

legendary.name: Children of the Stars
entries: Void dragons drift through the empty spaces beyond the boundaries of the mortal world and wander between the stars. They are aloof, mingling only with the otherworldly beings that live above and beyond the world, including the incarnate forms of the stars themselves.

legendary.name: Witnesses to the Void
entries: Void dragons are intensely knowledgeable creatures, but they have seen too much, lingering at the edge of the Void itself. They carry a piece of that nothing with them, and it flows out of them to break the minds of lesser beings when the dragons fly into a rage.

legendary.name: Voracious Scholars
entries: Despite their removed existence and strange quirks, void dragons still hoard treasure. Gems that glitter like the stars of their home are particularly prized. Their crowning piece, however, is knowledge. Void dragons jealously hoard scraps of forbidden and forgotten lore of any kind and spend most of their time at home poring over these treasures. A VOID DRAGON’S LAIR Void dragons make their lairs deep in the freezing, airless space between the stars. When a void dragon claims a home elsewhere, it prefers towering mountain peaks, valleys, or ruins at high elevation with a clear view of the sky. When encountered in its lair, an adult void dragon has a challenge rating of 17 , and an ancient void dragon has a challenge rating of 23. LAIR ACTIONS On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), the dragon can take a lair action to cause one of the following effects; the dragon can’t use the same effect two rounds in a row:
• Orb of the Void. The Void briefly overlaps the dragon’s

lair in a 20-foot-radius sphere of blackness punctuated by deep blue streaks and pinpoints of light. The sphere is centered on a point the dragon can see within 120 feet of the dragon. The sphere spreads around corners, is heavily obscured, and contains no air (creatures must hold their breath). Each creature in the sphere when it appears must make a DC 15 CON save, taking 7 (2d6) cold damage and 7 (2d6) necrotic damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one. Any creature that ends its turn in the sphere takes 7 (2d6) cold damage and 7 (2d6) necrotic damage. The sphere lasts 1 minute, until the dragon uses this lair action again, or until the dragon dies.
• Spatial Anomaly. The lair’s connection to the Void
briefly rips the fabric of space, forcing two creatures the dragon can see within 120 feet of it to suddenly exist at

entries: Creatures resistant to necrotic damage aren’t resistant to necrotic damage dealt by the void dragon. A creature immune to necrotic damage is still immune to the dragon’s necrotic damage.

trait.name: Void Dweller
entries: The void dragon doesn’t require air, food, drink, sleep, or ambient pressure, and it is immune to the effects of Void terrain. While traveling in space, the dragon magically glides on solar winds, covering immense distances in short times.

energy of the Void in a 15-foot cone. Each creature in that area must make a DC 13 DEX save, taking 21 (6d6) necrotic damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

The creature can be restored to life only by means of a true resurrection or a wish spell.

reaction.name: Text
entries: Dragon, White The wings and pearlescent scales of this dragon are tinged with blue, and its eyes sparkle an icy blue as it opens its jaw and exhales a blast of wintry air.

reaction.name: Primeval Hunters
entries: Animalistic and instinct-driven, white dragons are as much a force of nature as the blizzards of their arctic homes. They hunt for sport, killing more than they can eat and storing frozen kills as snacks for later. When faced with greater challenges, white dragons fight dirty, using slippery ice, sheer glacial cliffs, and furious snowstorms or spring thunderstorms to their advantage against intruders.

reaction.name: Vainglorious
entries: White dragons think highly of themselves and readily boast about their achievements to other dragons, especially other white dragons. Because of this, white dragons often seek out powerful opponents, as the more powerful a slain opponent, the more a dragon can boast to its brethren. Clever adventurers that speak with a white dragon can play to the dragon’s vanity, praising its skill and prowess or challenging it to perform seemingly impossible tasks.

reaction.name: Trophy Hoards
entries: T rophies are important to white dragons and are integral parts of their hoards. The bodies of frozen foes, rent and battered armor, bent weapons, and similar items showcasing the dragon’s accomplishments fill its lair. White dragons have little use or concern for sparkling treasure, but they recognize when a creature goes to great lengths to protect something. They proudly display items taken from creatures that gave their lives to protect the items, not always understanding the significance of the item but taking pride in slaying its protector. This leads white dragon lairs to be filled with an eclectic collection of weapons, religious relics, armor, and magic items.

reaction.name: Maturing
entries: White dragons care for their young for the first year of a wyrmling’s life, teaching the wyrmling how to hunt, store frozen prey, and create trophies. Eventually, however, the wyrmling’s growing sense of self and self- importance clashes with the egos of its parents, and all part ways. By the time the dragon is young, it has carved out an icy cave for itself and claimed a small swath of arctic or high mountain terrain as its territory. Adult white dragons spend their days expanding and affirming claim to their territory, often in the form of leaving slaughtered enemies scattered across the landscape. At this age, they also join in gatherings of white dragons, where they boast and display their battle prowess and where adults make alliances for raising strong young. White dragons live violent lives and don’t always survive to become ancients. Paragons of battle, violence, and pride, ancient white dragons are twice as deadly as any blizzard and half as merciful. A WHITE DRAGON’S LAIR A white dragon’s lair is carefully chosen for defensibility and remoteness. A series of icy caverns within a glacier, a cave on a high, sheer cliff face, or the ruins of a frost giant fortress atop a tall mountain are all prime choices for a lair. White dragons also like lairs where they can use their terrain tricks against intruders. Lairs with water are especially desirable, as the dragon can hide in a lake, pool, or stream and take intruders by surprise. They may dig tunnels from one room to another, useful either to withdraw or to cut off escape. LAIR ACTIONS On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), the dragon can take a lair action to cause one of the following effects; the dragon can’t use the same effect two rounds in a row:
• Blizzard. Whipping winds and snow appear in the

shape of a 20-foot-radius, 40-foot-high cylinder centered on a point the dragon can see within 120 feet of it. The blizzard’s area is heavily obscured. A creature that enters the blizzard for the first time on a turn or starts its turn there must succeed on a DC 15 CON save or take 7 (2d6) cold damage. A creature flying in or through the blizzard must land on its turn or be knocked prone.
• Frozen Treat. One creature the dragon can see within

60 feet of it begins to freeze solid. The target must make a DC 15 CON save. On a success, the target is restrained until the end of its next turn. On a failure, the target is petrified in ice for 1 minute. A petrified creature can repeat the save at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success. A petrified creature that takes fire damage has advantage on the save to end the effect.
• Ice Slick. A 40-foot radius patch of ground centered on

a point the dragon can see within 120 feet of it freezes. The ice slick is difficult terrain, and each time a creature moves more than 5 feet in or through the slick must succeed on a DC 15 DEX save or fall prone. The ice slick lasts for 1 minute, until the dragon uses this lair action again, or until the dragon dies.
• Thundershift. The weather in the dragon’s lair

suddenly shifts, causing a thunderous explosion on a point the dragon can see within 90 feet of it. Each creature within 20 feet of that point must make a DC 15 CON save. On a failure, a creature takes 7 (2d6) cold damage and 7 (2d6) thunder damage and is knocked prone. On a success, a creature takes half the damage and isn’t knocked prone. REGIONAL EFFECTS The region containing a white dragon’s lair is warped by its magic, which creates one or more of the following effects:
• Frigid Cold. Blizzards continually rage within 3
miles of the dragon’s lair, making the area nearly inhospitable for creatures not resistant to cold damage.
• Hunting Grounds. The area within 5 miles of the
dragon’s lair is its hunting ground. When a Large or larger creature enters the area, the dragon immediately knows the direction and general distance to that creature for the next hour.
• Icy Land. Ice and hard-packed snow coat the area
within 1 mile of the lair, making it difficult terrain. If the dragon dies, these effects fade in 1d10 days.

entries: Each creature of the dragon’s choice that is within 120 feet of the dragon and aware of it must succeed on a DC 17 WIS save or be frightened for 1 minute. A creature can repeat the save at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success. If a creature’s save is successful or the effect ends for it, the creature is immune to the dragon’s Frightful Presence for the next 24 hours.

action.name: Cold Breath (Recharge 5–6)
entries: The dragon exhales an icy blast in a 90-foot cone. Each creature in that area must make a DC 22 CON save, taking 63 (14d8) cold damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

legendary.name: Text
entries: The dragon can take 3 legendary actions, choosing from the options below. Only one legendary action can be used at a time and only at the end of another creature’s turn. The dragon regains spent legendary actions at the start of its turn.

legendary.name: Detect
entries: The dragon automatically detects every creature and trap within 30 feet of it, pinpointing the current location of each.

legendary.name: Tail Attack
entries: The dragon makes a Tail attack.

legendary.name: Elemental Roar (Costs 2 Actions)
entries: The dragon roars at up to two creatures of its choice within 120 feet of it. Each target must succeed on a DC 17 CHA save or be vulnerable to cold damage until the end of its next turn. A target resistant to cold damage isn’t resistant to it for the duration. A target immune to cold damage is still immune to it, even if the target fails the save.

legendary.name: Wing Attack (Costs 2 Actions)
entries: The dragon beats its wings. Each creature within 15 feet of the dragon must succeed on a DC 22 DEX save or take 15 (2d6 + 8) bludgeoning damage and be knocked prone. The dragon can then fly up to half its flying speed without provoking opportunity attacks. Dragon, Yellow A shadow ripples the tall grass as a dragon leaps from concealment, its yellow, catlike body blending into the dry grass. It opens its fanged mouth, which glows like the sun.

legendary.name: Grassland Dragons
entries: Y ellow dragons are solitary hunters in grasslands, hills, and canyons. Their bodies are low-slung, lithe, and catlike—perfect for stalking prey under the cover of tall grass or scrubland foliage. When young, their scales are a buttery yellow, but as they age, they turn a deeper yellow with flecks of tan. The oldest have scales the yellow-gold hue of the late-afternoon sun. This likeness to the sun isn’t superficial—a yellow dragon’s breath weapon is a searing beam of sunlight.

legendary.name: Consummate Hunters
entries: Y ellow dragons aren’t as animalistic and instinct-driven as their white dragon cousins, but they enjoy days filled with hunting and lounging in the sun, caring little for many of the worldly concerns that plague other dragons. They hunt for sport and like to make it a game, playing with their prey to make the hunt more enjoyable. They crave the chase most of all, and a yellow dragon that isn’t particularly hungry often releases caught prey and resumes the chase. Though winged, yellow dragons find the greatest pleasure in hunting on foot, catching prey unawares in the tall grasses.

legendary.name: Ever-Changing Hoards
entries: Y ellow dragons are easily distracted, mercurial creatures, and the trinket that held their attention last week might be this week’s junk. They find more delight in novelty rather than preening over the same treasures day after day. Because of this, many yellow dragons maintain friendly relations with local communities of grasslands goblins. The dragons’ propensity to forget or discard yesterday’s treasures complements the kleptomania that strikes many goblins, ensuring the dragon’s hoard never stays the same and forever holds its interest.

legendary.name: Maturing
entries: Y ellow dragon wyrmlings spend their first few years learning from their parents, who teach the wyrmling how to hunt, where to hunt in lean seasons, and how to avoid grasslands dangers like wildfires and other predators. When a yellow dragon is young, it establishes its own hunting territory on the outskirts of its progenitors’ territories, which includes at least one large rock or outcropping for sunning itself. Adult yellow dragons spend their days alternating between lounging in the warm sun and chasing various creatures in their territory, either to eat or for sport. By the time a yellow dragon becomes ancient, it often has an established relationship with local grasslands peoples, particularly goblins, providing protection in exchange for regular tribute of shiny trinkets or new prey. A YELLOW DRAGON’S LAIR A yellow dragon favors stone caverns near grasslands or in canyons—secure places they can retire to and rest when not hunting. These lairs often contain areas of sunbaked stone, which the dragons use to warm themselves, and a lair typically has at least one area with access to direct sunlight. The interior of a yellow dragon’s lair is often riddled with twisting tunnels that it can easily traverse with its lithe body. Most lairs contain several ambush points and even cunningly crafted hazards and traps designed to provide maximum enjoyment while hunting prey within its lair. LAIR ACTIONS On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), a yellow dragon can take a lair action to cause one of the following effects; the dragon can’t use the same effect two rounds in a row:
• Grasslands Cacophony. The sounds of thunder, howling

wind, insects, and a chorus of rustling grasses suddenly roar from a point the dragon can see within 60 feet of it. Each creature within 15 feet of that point that can hear the cacophony must succeed on a DC 15 DEX save or be deafened and stunned until the end of its next turn.
• Pitfall. A 20-foot-radius pit that is 20 feet deep opens

in the ground centered on a point the dragon can see within 120 feet of it. Each creature in that area must succeed on a DC 15 DEX save or fall into the pit, taking falling damage as normal and landing prone. The pit remains for 1 minute, until the dragon uses this lair action again, or until the dragon dies. When the pit ends, its floor rises up to the level of the ground, harmlessly pushing any creatures in the pit back to the surface.
• Radiant Flare. A flash of bright light bursts from a point

in the air the dragon can see within 120 feet of it. Each creature within 30 feet of it that can see the light must succeed on a DC 15 CON save or be blinded until the end of its next turn. REGIONAL EFFECTS The region containing a yellow dragon’s lair is changed by its presence, which creates one or more of the following effects:
• Calm Fires. Wildfires are less common within 10 miles
of the dragon’s lair, and when a wildfire burns within 10 miles of the lair, it lasts half as long.
• Fleeing Prey. Animals, especially prey animals, within
3 miles of the dragon’s lair are more skittish than normal and frequently run at the slightest movement or perceived danger.
• Intense Sunlight. Within 1 mile of the dragon’s

lair, the sun beats down more intensely than usual. During the day, each creature in the area, other than yellow dragons, must succeed on a DC 10 CON save every hour or suffer one level of exhaustion. A creature automatically succeeds on this save if it takes shelter from the sun or otherwise avoids direct sunlight. If the dragon dies, these effects fade in 1d10 days.

trait.name: Grasslands Camouflage
entries: The yellow dragon’s Stealth is 19 while in tall grass or foliage.

trait.name: Pounce
entries: If the dragon moves at least 15 feet straight toward a creature and then hits with a Claw attack on the same turn, that target must succeed on a DC 12 STR save or be knocked prone. If the target is prone, the dragon can make one Claw attack against it as a bonus action.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The dragon makes one Bite attack and one Claw attack.

action.name: Bite
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 8 (1d10 + 3) piercing damage plus 4 (1d8) radiant damage.

action.name: Claw
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 6 (1d6 + 3) slashing damage.

action.name: Radiant Breath (Recharge 5–6)
entries: The dragon exhales radiant energy in a 30-foot line that is 5 feet wide. Each creature in the line must make a DC 12 DEX save, taking 27 (6d8) radiant damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

trait.name: Blinding Sunlight
entries: When the dragon hasn’t released its Radiant Breath, streaks of sunlight pour from its mouth. Bright light within 10 feet of the dragon is sunlight, and each creature that starts its turn within 10 feet of the dragon must succeed on a DC 15 CON save or be blinded until the start of its next turn.

trait.name: Grasslands Camouflage
entries: The yellow dragon’s Stealth is 20 while in tall grass or foliage.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The dragon makes one Bite attack and two Claw attacks.

action.name: Bite
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +8 to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. Hit: 16 (2d10 + 5) piercing damage plus 4 (1d8) radiant damage.

action.name: Claw
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +8 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 12 (2d6 + 5) slashing damage.

Bright light within 20 feet of the dragon is sunlight, and each creature that starts its turn within 20 feet of the dragon must succeed on a DC 19 CON save or be blinded until the start of its next turn.

trait.name: Grasslands Camouflage
entries: The yellow dragon’s Stealth is 22 while in tall grass or foliage.

trait.name: Legendary Resistance (3/Day)
entries: If the yellow dragon fails a save, it can choose to succeed instead.

action.name: Multiattack

action.name: Claw

If a creature’s save is successful or the effect ends for it, the creature is immune to the dragon’s Frightful Presence for the next 24 hours.

action.name: Claw

legendary.name: Text
entries: The dragon can take 3 legendary actions, choosing from the options below. Only one legendary action can be used at a time and only at the end of another creature’s turn. The dragon regains spent legendary actions at the start of its turn.

legendary.name: Detect

legendary.name: Tail Attack

legendary.name: Wing Attack (Costs 2 Actions)
entries: The dragon beats its wings. Each creature within 10 feet of the dragon must succeed on a DC 23 DEX save or take 16 (2d6 + 9) bludgeoning damage and be knocked prone. The dragon can then fly up to half its flying speed without provoking opportunity attacks. Dragonette, Pseudodragon This cat-sized dragon maintains a cautious crouch, candlelight flickering across its rust-red scales. It raises its stinger-tipped tail defensively before it.

legendary.name: Miniature Dragon
entries: These small cousins of true dragons dwell in forests, lairing in hollow trees, stumps, small caves, or other snug, enclosed areas. While they resemble miniature red dragons, their fine scales are a reddish-brown in color, and their tails end in a venomous stinger. Pseudodragons are also much less aggressive and more skittish than their larger cousins. When desperate, they can deliver a nasty bite with their needle-sharp teeth, but they rely on their stingers for hunting prey and defending themselves, as their venom can incapacitate even large opponents.

legendary.name: Telepathic
entries: Pseudodragons possess a limited form of telepathy that communicates simple emotions or states to others, such as agreement, anxiety, discomfort, acceptance, hunger, or similar. These communications can also take the form of a series of images.

legendary.name: Favored Familiars
entries: Wizards consider pseudodragons quite desirable familiars. Their bonding is more complicated than garnering a standard familiar, because pseudodragons view this as a partnership rather than servitude. They often must be persuaded with offerings or bribes.

monster.name: Pseudodragon
languages: understands Common and Draconic but can’t speak

trait.name: Heightened Senses
entries: The pseudodragon’s Perception is 18 when perceiving by sight, hearing, or smell.

trait.name: Magic Resistance
entries: The pseudodragon has advantage on saves against spells and other magical effects.

trait.name: Limited Telepathy
entries: The pseudodragon can magically communicate simple ideas, emotions, and images telepathically with any creature within 100 feet of it that can understand a language.

action.name: Bite
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +4 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 4 (1d4 + 2) piercing damage.

action.name: Sting
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +4 to hit, reach 5 ft., one creature. Hit: 4 (1d4 + 2) piercing damage plus 2 (1d4) poison damage, and the target must succeed on a DC 11 CON save or be poisoned for 1 hour. If the creature fails the save by 5 or more, it falls unconscious while poisoned in this way, or until it takes damage or another creature uses an action to shake it awake.

action.name: Puff of Smoke (1/Day)
entries: The pseudodragon exhales a cloud of smoke in a 10-foot-radius sphere centered on itself. The sphere spreads around corners, and its area is heavily obscured. It lasts for 1 minute or until a wind of moderate or greater speed (at least 10 miles per hour) disperses it. Drake, Scorch The dragon’s body is covered in shimmering scales of red, orange, and yellow. Its eyes glow like hot coals and fire burns in its throat as it roars.

action.name: Roasted Prey
entries: The scorch drake roams charred wastelands and volcanic plains, hunting anything it can catch. Unable to digest fresh kills, scorch drakes roast victims with their fiery breath before dining. They often scavenge prey baked or cooked by other means, such as geothermal activity, the fire breath of other dragons, or even a desiccated body left for days in the sun. In this way, they conserve energy, reserving their potent fire breath for only the choicest meals.

action.name: Roaming Hunter
entries: Scorch drakes often leave their stony wasteland homes to find more plentiful prey, moving into neighboring grasslands or forests. However, their hunting style often turns those fertile lands into charred wastes. Because they are unbothered by heat and fire, they often start wildfires to flush out prey, gobbling up scorched remains or ambushing prey fleeing advancing flames.

action.name: Liquid Fire
entries: Scorch drakes radiate heat and are difficult to see clearly at a distance due to the shimmer the heat creates. They exhale a spray of liquid that ignites upon contact with air, which resembles the fire breath of other dragons. However, this liquid is more akin to alchemist’s fire, and it coats and clings to the drake’s prey, causing victims to continue burning.

trait.name: Heat Shimmer
entries: Creatures more than 30 feet from the scorch drake have disadvantage on WIS (Perception) checks to see the drake.

trait.name: Scalding Blood
entries: A creature that hits the scorch drake with a melee attack while within 5 feet of the drake takes 3 (1d6) fire damage and must succeed on a DC 15 DEX save or ignite. Until a creature takes an action to douse the fire, an ignited creature takes 3 (1d6) fire damage at the start of each of its turns.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The scorch drake makes one Bite and two Claw attacks.

action.name: Bite
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 12 (2d8 + 3) piercing and 3 (1d6) fire damage.

action.name: Claw
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 8 (2d4 + 3) slashing and 3 (1d6) fire damage.

action.name: Fire Breath (Recharge 5–6)
entries: The scorch drake spits liquid fire in a 30-foot cone. Each creature in that area must make a DC 15 DEX save. On a failure, a creature takes 24 (7d6) fire damage and ignites. On a success, a creature takes half the damage and doesn’t ignite. Until a creature takes an action to douse the fire, an ignited creature takes 3 (1d6) fire damage at the start of each of its turns. This fire ignites flammable objects in the line that aren’t being worn or carried.

bonus.name: Fan the Flames
entries: The scorch drake flaps its wings in the direction of one ignited creature it can see within 15 feet of it. The flames coating the target flare up and generate excess smoke. The target must succeed on a DC 15 CON save or be blinded until the end of its next turn. Drider The drow smiles, its eyes gleaming silver in the darkness. Six more points of silver appear on its forehead as it lowers itself from the cavern ceiling, revealing its arachnid body.

bonus.name: Abyssal Origins
entries: The first driders were drow elves who wronged the demon lord of spiders and were transformed as punishment for their transgressions. They have since become their own people that inhabit the dark caverns of the world. Driders sometimes ally with drow, duergar, and other denizens of the underworld, living alongside the smaller folk in their settlements, but driders most often keep to themselves in tight-knit family groups.

bonus.name: Underworld Centaurs
entries: Like their surface-world mirrors, driders roam the underworld and are suited for its environment. They can climb walls and traverse ceilings without effort, and where many centaurs have human features, driders nearly always have elven features.

bonus.name: Arachnid Bodies
entries: Driders’ lower bodies more than simply resemble spiders; they also have many arachnid features. Their abdomens contain spinnerets for creating webbing, their legs contain sensory organs for detecting movement on webs, and their foremost legs contain poison, like spider mandibles.

trait.name: Fey Ancestry
entries: The drider is resistant to the charmed condition, and magic can’t put the drider to sleep.

trait.name: Monstrosity Resilience
entries: The drider is resistant to exhaustion and to the frightened condition.

trait.name: Spider Climb
entries: The drider can climb difficult surfaces, including upside down on ceilings, without needing to make an ability check.

trait.name: Sunlight Sensitivity
entries: While in sunlight, the drider has disadvantage on attack rolls, and its Perception is 10 when perceiving by sight.

trait.name: Web Sense
entries: While in contact with a web, the drider knows the exact location of any other creature in contact with the same web.

trait.name: Web Walker
entries: The drider ignores movement restrictions caused by webbing.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The drider makes one Longsword attack and two Skewer attacks, or it makes three Web Shot attacks.

action.name: Longsword
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 7 (1d8 + 3) slashing damage, or 8 (1d10 + 3) slashing damage if used with two hands.

action.name: Skewer
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one creature. Hit: 6 (1d6 + 3) piercing damage plus 13 (3d8) poison damage.

action.name: Web Shot
entries: Ranged Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, range 30/60 ft., one target. Hit: 14 (2d10 + 3) bludgeoning damage, and the target must succeed on a DC 15 STR save or be restrained by webbing. A creature, including the restrained target, can take its action to break the webbing and free the restrained target by succeeding on a DC 15 STR check. Dryad A green woman with barklike skin and bedecked in vibrant green leaves and vines emerges from an ancient oak tree.

action.name: Forest Defenders
entries: Dryads are fey that are magically bound to trees in the oldest forests of the world, becoming extensions of those trees and defenders of the forest. The death of a dryad’s tree usually results in the death of the dryad, unless the tree’s death was part of the tree’s natural life cycle. A dryad that has survived the death of its tree has one year to find a new, suitable tree with which to bond, or it perishes.

action.name: Wildlife Allies
entries: When in danger, dryads call on the forest’s animals to protect them or cover their retreat. Dryads can also magically convince would-be foresters and hunters to leave the forest in peace or to seek wood and prey elsewhere. This often leads to conflicting stories of dryads as beautiful and charming or ferocious and accompanied by armies of savage wildlife.

action.name: Helping Lost Travelers
entries: Dryads aren’t always inimical to creatures hailing from outside their forests. They often help creatures lost in the woods, especially children or humanoids that show respect to nature. Dryads understand that kindness shown to lost travelers today might lead to broader respect and care for the forest in the future.

trait.name: Fey Resilience
entries: The dryad is resistant to the charmed and unconscious conditions.

trait.name: Magic Resistance
entries: The dryad has advantage on saves against spells and other magical effects.

trait.name: One with the Forest
entries: While in a forest, the dryad can’t be surprised, and it leaves behind no tracks or other traces of its passage.

trait.name: Speak with Beasts and Plants
entries: The dryad can communicate with Beasts and Plants as if they shared a language.

trait.name: Tree Stride
entries: Once on its turn, the dryad can use 10 feet of its movement to step magically into one living tree within its reach and emerge from a second living tree within 60 feet of the first tree, appearing in an unoccupied space within 5 feet of the second tree. Both trees must be Large or larger.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The dryad makes two Vine Whip or Blast of Pollen attacks. If both Vine Whip attacks hit one creature, the target must succeed on a DC 14 STR save or be restrained by vines until the end of its next turn.

action.name: Vine Whip
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +4 to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. Hit: 6 (1d8 + 2) slashing damage.

action.name: Blast of Pollen
entries: Ranged Spell Attack: +6 to hit, range 60 ft., one target. Hit: 7 (1d6 + 4) poison damage.

bonus.name: Charming Scent
entries: The dryad emits an alluring, floral scent toward one Beast or Humanoid it can see within 30 feet of it. The target must succeed on a DC 14 WIS save or be charmed for 1 minute. While charmed, the target views the dryad as an ally and views the dryad’s requests favorably, though it isn’t under the dryad’s control. The charmed creature can repeat the save at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success. The dryad can have up to two creatures charmed in this way at a time. Forest’s Aid (21 HP or Fewer, 1/Day). The dryad magically calls a horde of squirrels, rabbits, mice, and other small animals native to the forest. The horde uses the statistics of 1 swarm of rats. The called creatures arrive on initiative count 20 of the next round, acting as allies of the dryad and obeying its spoken commands. They remain for 1 hour, until the dryad dies, or until the dryad dismisses them as a bonus action. Dwarf, Duergar The milky white eyes of the stony-skinned dwarf scan the nearby caverns. It then slams its large pick into the cavern wall, knocking loose a bit of metal ore, which it tosses into a nearby cart.

bonus.name: Militaristic Dwarves
entries: As dwarves who believed in martial law over a sense of community, the duergar broke away to form their own settlements, traveling deeper into the underworld and away from other dwarves. Their society is comprised of castes, and movement up or down depends on an individual’s skills and contribution to the military, including military service. Duergar didn’t fully forsake the family units of their overworld cousins, but replaced them with bonds within their military family. Many platoons of troops form lifelong bonds and raise children together.

bonus.name: Utilitarian Wares
entries: Duergar craft supports their army, from defensible architecture, to weapons that rarely dull, to gleaming armor. Preferring function over form, duergar goods rarely feature embellishments or designs.

bonus.name: Bastion Against Horrors
entries: When they broke away from their overworld cousins, duergar didn’t fully embrace a militaristic society until they encountered the denizens of the underworld: abyssal fiends, ghoulish undead, aberrant monstrosities, and many other reality-bending horrors. Duergar regularly battle these horrors, seeing the near-endless struggle as a test of their hardiness and commitment to their life below ground, and they have learned techniques, both to evade the horrors and increase their size to go toe-to-toe with the beasts.

trait.name: Duergar Resilience
entries: The duergar is resistant to the charmed and paralyzed conditions, and it has advantage on saves against spells and illusions.

trait.name: Reduce
entries: If the duergar starts its turn enlarged, it can choose to end the effect and return to its normal size (no action required).

trait.name: Sunlight Sensitivity
entries: While in sunlight, the duergar has disadvantage on attack rolls, and its Perception is 5 when perceiving by sight.

action.name: War Pick
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +4 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 6 (1d8 + 2) piercing damage, or 11 (2d8 + 2) piercing damage while enlarged.

action.name: Javelin
entries: Melee or Ranged Weapon Attack: +4 to hit, reach 5 ft. or range 30/120 ft., one target. Hit: 5 (1d6 + 2) piercing damage, or 9 (2d6 + 2) piercing damage while enlarged.

action.name: Invisibility
entries: The duergar magically turns invisible until it attacks or uses Enlarge, or until its concentration ends (as if concentrating on a spell). Any equipment the duergar wears or carries is invisible with it.

bonus.name: Enlarge
entries: The duergar magically increases in size, along with any equipment it is wearing or carrying. While enlarged, the duergar is Large, doubles its damage dice on weapon attacks (included in the attacks), and makes STR checks and saves with advantage. While enlarged, it also can no longer use the Invisibility action. If the duergar lacks the room to become Large, this bonus action fails.

reaction.name: Shield Wall
entries: The duergar adds 2 to its AC against one weapon attack that would hit it. To do so, the duergar must be wielding a shield and within 5 feet of another duergar that is also wielding a shield. Elemental, Air A cyclone howls as it picks up and scatters debris before settling in a vaguely humanoid shape.

reaction.name: Sentient Storms
entries: Air elementals are gathered collections of air, wind, and planar material from the Plane of Air powered by an energetic intelligence. They are most at home on the Plane of Air, but they often find themselves slipping into the Material Plane when a spellcaster summons them or when great thunderstorms on the Material Plane briefly split the barrier between the two planes .

reaction.name: Always Moving
entries: Air elementals are full of energy and rarely stay stationary. An air elemental having a conversation might dart around its conversational partner, punctuating each sentence with a halt and tiny thunderclap. When engaging in combat, air elementals similarly dart around their opponents, pausing long enough to attack with a lash of wind or surround and toss opponents.

trait.name: Air Form
entries: The elemental can enter a hostile creature’s space and stop there. It can move through a space as narrow as 1 inch wide without squeezing.

trait.name: Cloud Camouflage
entries: The elemental’s Stealth is 20 while in clouds, fog, or windy weather.

trait.name: Elemental Nature
entries: The elemental doesn’t require air, food, drink, or sleep.

trait.name: Elemental Resilience
entries: The elemental is resistant to bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing damage from nonmagical attacks. In addition, it is immune to poison damage, to exhaustion, and to the grappled, paralyzed, petrified, poisoned, prone, restrained, and unconscious conditions.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The elemental makes two Wind Lash or Lightning Bolt attacks.

action.name: Wind Lash
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +8 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 18 (3d8 + 5) bludgeoning damage.

action.name: Lightning Bolt
entries: Ranged Weapon Attack: +8 to hit, range 30/120 ft., one target. Hit: 18 (3d8 + 5) lightning damage.

action.name: Whirlwind (Recharge 4–6)
entries: Each creature in the elemental’s space must make a DC 13 STR save. On a failure, a target takes 18 (3d8 + 5) bludgeoning damage and is flung up 20 feet away from the elemental in a random direction and knocked prone. If a thrown target strikes an object, such as a wall or floor, the target takes 3 (1d6) bludgeoning damage for every 10 feet it was thrown. If the target is thrown at another creature, that creature must succeed on a DC 13 DEX save or take the same damage and be knocked prone. If the save is successful, the target takes half the bludgeoning damage and isn’t flung away or knocked prone.

reaction.name: Thunderous Retreat
entries: When a creature the air elemental can see deals damage to it, the elemental can expel a crack of thunder and move up to 15 feet to an unoccupied space it can see. Each creature within 5 feet of the space the elemental left must succeed on a DC 13 CON save or take 7 (2d6) thunder damage. Elemental, Earth The ground rumbles and shifts, forming two large fists, then a pair of legs, then a small head with gemstone eyes and a cavernous mouth.

reaction.name: Sentient Soil
entries: Earth elementals are collections of soil, rock, gemstones, and planar material from the Plane of Earth powered by a quiet intelligence. They are equally at home on the Plane of Earth and the Material Plane, dwelling in the depths of the soil and rock of both planes.

reaction.name: Plodding Pace
entries: Earth elementals move and change as quickly as the earth around them, often lying comfortably for decades in the same location. When circumstances force them to act, however, they act with powerful swings of their rocky fists, and the very earth around them rumbles and moves at their call. Many would-be bandits have discovered too late that some small mountain villages are unknowingly protected by earth elementals when they attempt to harm villagers, who incidentally tended plants on the elemental’s back for decades or who once sang to the elemental years ago.

trait.name: Earth Glide
entries: The elemental can burrow through nonmagical, unworked earth and stone. While doing so, the elemental doesn’t disturb the material through which it moves.

trait.name: Earthen Camouflage
entries: The elemental’s Stealth is 14 while in muddy, rocky, sandy, or stony terrain.

trait.name: Elemental Nature
entries: The elemental doesn’t require air, food, drink, or sleep.

trait.name: Elemental Resilience
entries: The elemental is resistant to bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing damage from nonmagical attacks. In addition, it is immune to poison damage, to exhaustion, and to the paralyzed, petrified, poisoned, and unconscious conditions.

trait.name: Siege Monster
entries: The elemental deals double damage to objects and structures.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The elemental makes three Slam attacks, or it makes two Lob Stone attacks. If two Slam or Lob Stone attacks hit one creature, the target must succeed on a DC 16 STR save or be knocked prone.

action.name: Slam
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +8 to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. Hit: 14 (2d8 + 5) bludgeoning damage.

action.name: Lob Stone
entries: Ranged Weapon Attack: +8 to hit, range 20/60 ft., one target. Hit: 21 (3d10 + 5) bludgeoning damage.

bonus.name: Earth Tremor (Recharge 4–6)
entries: The earth elemental causes the ground on a point it can see within 30 feet of it to rumble and shake violently. Each creature within 10 feet of that point must succeed on a DC 16 DEX save or be knocked prone. Elemental, Fire This mobile flame casts light in varying hues of yellow and orange, setting flammable objects alight as it touches them.

bonus.name: Sentient Fire
entries: Fire elementals are living fire from the Plane of Fire powered by an irascible intelligence. They are most at home on the Plane of Fire, but they often make their way to the Material Plane when a spellcaster summons them, when large wildfires rage across grasslands, or when volcanic eruptions burst small holes between the two planes.

bonus.name: Blazing Trails
entries: Born from a plane filled with fire and heat, fire elementals—sometimes accidentally, sometimes deliberately—set fires everywhere they go, finding comfort in the fire. When unable to set fires or told they can’t, they often become angered and blaze even hotter. Clever negotiators often encourage fire elementals to find comfort in warm areas of the world where their fire would be less destructive, such as active volcanoes or desert sands.

trait.name: Elemental Nature
entries: The fire elemental doesn’t require air, food, drink, or sleep.

trait.name: Elemental Resilience
entries: The fire elemental is resistant to bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing damage from nonmagical attacks. In addition, it is immune to poison damage, to exhaustion, and to the grappled, paralyzed, petrified, poisoned, prone, restrained, and unconscious conditions.

trait.name: Fire Camouflage
entries: The fire elemental’s Stealth is 18 while in a Medium or larger fire.

trait.name: Fire Form
entries: The elemental can move through a space as narrow as 1 inch wide without squeezing. A creature that touches the elemental or hits it with a melee attack while within 5 feet of it takes 5 (1d10) fire damage. In addition, the elemental can enter a hostile creature’s space and stop there. The first time it enters a creature’s space on a turn, that creature must succeed on a DC 14 DEX save or take 5 (1d10) fire damage and catch fire; until a creature takes an action to douse the fire, the burning creature takes 5 (1d10) fire damage at the start of each of its turns.

trait.name: Illumination
entries: The elemental sheds bright light in a 30-foot radius and dim light for an additional 30 feet.

trait.name: Water Susceptibility
entries: For every 5 feet the elemental moves in water, or for every gallon of water splashed on it, it takes 1 cold damage.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The water elemental makes two Water Tendril or Water Bolt attacks. If both Water Tendril attacks hit one creature, it can use Whelm on the target, if available.

action.name: Water Tendril
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +7 to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. Hit: 17 (3d8 + 4) bludgeoning damage, and the target is grappled (escape DC 15) if it is Large or smaller. The water elemental can have up to three targets grappled at a time.

action.name: Water Bolt
entries: Ranged Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, range 30/120 ft., one target. Hit: 16 (4d6 + 2) bludgeoning damage.

action.name: Whelm (Recharge 4–6)
entries: The water elemental envelops one creature it is grappling, and the grapple ends. While enveloped, the target is restrained, unable to breathe unless it can breathe water, can’t be hit by the elemental’s Water Tendril, and it takes 9 (2d8) bludgeoning damage at the start of each of the elemental’s turns. When the elemental moves, the enveloped creature moves with it. The elemental can have only one creature enveloped at a time. A creature within 5 feet of the elemental, including the target, can take its action to free the target from the elemental by succeeding on a DC 15 STR check.

bonus.name: Purify Blood (3/Day)
entries: The water elemental touches a willing creature that isn’t a Construct or Undead. The target is cured of the poisoned condition and of any disease afflicting it. Elemental, Water A wave of water roils and rises, forming into a humanoid shape with a face that constantly disappears and reforms.

bonus.name: Sentient Water
entries: Water elementals are living water from the Plane of Water powered by a mercurial intelligence. They are equally at home on the Plane of Water and the Material Plane, and they can be found in the deepest ocean trenches, the hearts of springs, or the depths of large lakes on both planes.

bonus.name: Purest Water
entries: Made almost entirely of water from the Plane of Water, water elementals’ bodies are water in its purest form and are always free of salt, algae, tiny organisms, and other impurities. They despise creatures and actions that corrupt, poison, or otherwise befoul water. They can cleanse some impurities in the blood of creatures, and coastal communities often petition them for aid in times of plague.

trait.name: Elemental Nature
entries: The elemental doesn’t require air, food, drink, or sleep.

trait.name: Elemental Resilience
entries: The elemental is resistant to bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing damage from nonmagical attacks. In addition, it is immune to poison damage, to exhaustion, and to the grappled, paralyzed, petrified, poisoned, prone, restrained, and unconscious conditions.

trait.name: Freeze
entries: If the elemental takes cold damage, it partially freezes, and its speed is reduced by 20 feet until the end of its next turn.

trait.name: Purify Water
entries: If the water elemental sits in a body of slow-flowing or standing water, such as a spring, small creek, or lake, for at least 1 hour, the water becomes purified and rendered free of poison and disease. In slow-flowing water, this purification fades 1 hour after the elemental leaves the water. In standing water, this purification lasts until a contaminant enters the water while the elemental isn’t in it.

trait.name: Water Form
entries: The elemental can enter a hostile creature’s space and stop there. It can move through a space as narrow as 1 inch wide without squeezing. Elf, Drow The golden-eyed elf steps from the shadows near the cavern wall, its shortsword held at the ready.

trait.name: Children of an Elven Schism
entries: Elves, intrinsically tied to nature, grew to appreciate only the aspects they considered beautiful, such as flowers, butterflies, and songbirds. A vocal contingent, alarmed by the effects of this aestheticism, spoke up for the less appreciated creatures whose utility in nature was just as important, taking particular interest in insects and spiders. Unable to come to an agreement with the others of their kind, these elves, taking the name drow, left the sunlit forests for the cool, welcoming darkness of the underworld.

trait.name: Arachnid Motifs
entries: The drow revere and care for insects of all kinds but hold arachnids and the number eight especially sacred. Though many drow include spider motifs in their armor, weapons, and architecture, just as many use motifs of scorpions, beetles, mites, centipedes, ants, and other arachnids and insects. Some drow societies and settlements even mimic the structures of their chosen icons, from ant-like caste systems to web bridges between suspended buildings to short, round buildings with shell-like walls.

trait.name: Unique Poisons
entries: Because of their regular association with arachnids and insects, drow have developed a special appreciation for venoms and poisons. They gather venom from their beloved insects, create unique poisons from combinations of these venoms, and coat their weapons in the results when defending their homes.

trait.name: Fey Ancestry
entries: The drow is resistant to the charmed condition, and magic can’t put the drow to sleep.

trait.name: Sunlight Sensitivity
entries: While in sunlight, the drow has disadvantage on attack rolls, and its Perception is 7 when perceiving by sight.

action.name: Shortsword
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +4 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 5 (1d6 + 2) piercing damage.

action.name: Hand Crossbow
entries: Ranged Weapon Attack: +4 to hit, range 30/120 ft., one target. Hit: 5 (1d6 + 2) piercing damage.

bonus.name: Poison Weapon
entries: The drow coats one weapon it is wearing or carrying in one of the following poisons. Each time the drow hits a creature with that weapon, the target must succeed on a DC 11 CON save or be poisoned for 1 minute and suffer the chosen poison’s effect while poisoned in this way. The poisoned creature can repeat the save at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success. The poison remains on the weapon until the start of the drow’s next turn.
• Beetle’s Bite. The target’s speed is halved while poisoned.
• Dark Caress. The target is blinded while poisoned.
• Nightmare’s Kiss. The target is frightened of the drow
while poisoned.
• Slumber’s Call. The target falls unconscious while

poisoned or until it takes damage or another creature uses an action to wake it. Ettercap Webbing trails from the body of this arachnid humanoid. Its elongated limbs are tipped with chitinous claws, and its mandibles drip with foul liquid. Four additional spider legs sprout from its torso, helping it cling to the wall.

bonus.name: Cursed Druids
entries: Millennia ago, a sect of druids that revered spiders sought to create a closer bond between themselves and the spiders they loved. They performed a weeks-long, forbidden ritual that called on magic from the deity of insects. The ritual was intended to give the druids magical bonds with spiders and even allow them to adopt some spider-like features. Incensed at the audacity of the druids, the deity gave the druids what they wanted—and more. The druids were transformed into spider-like monstrosities, stripped of their druidic magic and much of their intelligence, and left in the depths of a jungle far from home or anything familiar.

bonus.name: Spider Friends
entries: Now known as ettercaps, the descendants of those ancient, cursed druids spend their days protecting and nurturing spiders in the jungles and forests of the world. Each ettercap acts as a clan leader of a collection or territory of spiders of all types, shapes, and sizes and ruthlessly defends its charges. Ettercaps exposed to humanoids often pick up enough of the local language to communicate basic ideas and needs. Some ettercaps even broker minor trade agreements of spidersilk in exchange for food, sustaining their colonies and maintaining peace between the spiders and locals.

trait.name: Monstrosity Resilience
entries: The ettercap is resistant to exhaustion and to the frightened condition.

trait.name: Spider Climb
entries: The ettercap can climb difficult surfaces, including upside down on ceilings, without needing to make an ability check.

trait.name: Web Sense
entries: While in contact with a web, the ettercap knows the exact location of any other creature in contact with the same web.

trait.name: Web Walker
entries: The ettercap ignores movement restrictions caused by webbing.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The ettercap makes one Bite attack and one Claws attack, or it makes two Spit Poison attacks.

action.name: Bite
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, reach 5 ft., one creature. Hit: 7 (1d8 + 3) piercing damage plus 4 (1d8) poison damage. The target must succeed on a DC 13 CON save or be poisoned for 1 minute. The target can repeat the save at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success.

action.name: Claws
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 10 (2d6 + 3) slashing damage.

action.name: Spit Poison
entries: Ranged Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, range 20/60 ft., one target. Hit: 11 (2d8 + 3) poison damage.

bonus.name: Web (Recharge 4–6)
entries: The ettercap launches a gob of webbing at one creature it can see within 30 feet of it. The target must succeed on a DC 13 DEX save or be restrained. A creature, including the restrained target, can take its action to free the target from the webbing by succeeding on a DC 13 STR check. The webbing can also be attacked and destroyed (AC 10; HP 5; vulnerability to fire damage; immunity to bludgeoning, poison, and psychic damage). Ettin The pair of heads atop the giant’s shoulders bicker with each other as it stomps through the sparse trees on the hillside. The argument is occasionally punctuated by thrusts and shakes of a club that is little more than a tree branch.

bonus.name: Lousy Roommates
entries: Ettins have an independent- minded brain in each of their heads, and they rarely get along with each other. They constantly argue with each other over what to eat, which way to travel, and various other trivialities. Each head controls its side of the body, except when asleep when control switches to the awake head. This disconnect between minds and halves of body leaves most ettins with a jerky, uneven gait. When presented with a credible threat or an empty belly, though, the two heads put aside their differences to vanquish it.

bonus.name: Two Heads Are Better Than One
entries: The obstinacy of the two heads makes it difficult for opponents to affect the ettin’s minds. Having two minds also protects the ettin from many effects that might make it unable to move or defend itself or that might otherwise disorient it.

bonus.name: With Harmony, Wisdom
entries: The constant bickering results in many ettins leading violent and often dangerous lives, as their squabbles attract the wrong attention or their heads’ stubbornness with each other puts them in deadly situations. The oldest ettins, however, have either built up the strength to face any danger they create for themselves or learn to reach a harmony between the heads. These older, harmonious ettins are a terror to behold on a battlefield and can be an invaluable source of wisdom for those studying mediation and the art of negotiation.

trait.name: Giant Attributes
entries: The ettin is resistant to the stunned condition, and it is vulnerable to the prone condition.

trait.name: Multiple Heads
entries: The ettin has two heads and is resistant to the blinded, charmed, deafened, frightened, stunned, and unconscious conditions.

trait.name: Obstinate
entries: The ettin has advantage on WIS and CHA saves.

trait.name: Wakeful
entries: When one of the ettin’s heads is asleep, its other head is awake.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The ettin makes one Knobbed Club attack and one Spiked Club attack.

action.name: Knobbed Club
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +7 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 18 (3d8 + 5) bludgeoning damage, and the target must succeed on a DC 15 STR save or be incapacitated until the end of its next turn, as the blow knocks the wind out of the target. This weapon is wielded by the right head.

action.name: Spiked Club
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +7 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 18 (3d8 + 5) piercing damage, and the target must succeed on a DC 15 CON save or be poisoned for 1 minute, as bits of rotten meat clinging to the club’s spikes coat the target’s wound. The target can repeat the save at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success. This weapon is wielded by the left head.

bonus.name: Text
entries: That One! One of the ettin’s heads picks a creature the ettin can see within 30 feet of it, gaining advantage on its next attack against that creature. The other head doesn’t like being told what to do and has disadvantage on its next attack against that creature. Each weapon attack notes which head controls that weapon. Feral Hunter A flash of bright color emerges from the forest’s emerald hues. The figure’s hair reflects the shimmering colors of the fey realms, and its face has a bestial aspect, with large ears and yellow, slitted eyes. It nocks an arrow, poised to strike.

bonus.name: Primal Fey
entries: Feral hunters are fey with a deep connection to the vibrant, primal power that courses through the Plane of the Fey. Their humanoid appearance is paired with bestial qualities, such as tails, large ears, horns, patches of fur, or slit or square pupils, and no two feral hunters look the same. All feral hunters have colorful fur and hair, which often shimmers when it is on the Material Plane.

bonus.name: Consummate Hunters
entries: The hunters’ primal connection to the fey realms makes them invaluable scouts and trackers to the archfey of the plane. An archfey might task a hunter with safely guiding visitors or with hunting down and killing intruders. On rare occasions, an archfey might send a feral hunter to track a mortal or fey who escaped the fey realms. The hunter is relentless in its pursuit and always returns with its prize or dies trying.

bonus.name: Call of the Wild
entries: Feral hunters regularly punctuate their speech with growls, snarls, and hisses. They rarely tolerate lengthy talking or politicking and may lash out violently when frustrated or annoyed. They feel more at home in the wilds of the Plane of the Fey, and even feral hunters serving archfey seldom stay in the courts of the archfey for long. Most visit only to deliver old bounties and collect new ones.

trait.name: Fey Resilience
entries: The feral hunter is resistant to the charmed and unconscious conditions.

trait.name: Heightened Senses
entries: The feral hunter’s Perception is 19 when perceiving by sight, hearing, or smell.

trait.name: Precise
entries: A weapon that deals piercing damage deals one extra die of its damage when the feral hunter hits with it (included in the attack).

trait.name: Woodland Walk
entries: Difficult terrain composed of nonmagical plants doesn’t cost the feral hunter extra movement. In addition, the feral hunter can pass through nonmagical plants without being slowed by them and without taking damage from them if they have thorns, spines, or a similar hazard.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The fey guardian makes two Rapier or Slam attacks, or it makes two Shooting Star attacks.

action.name: Rapier (Humanoid or True Form Only)
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 7 (1d8 + 3) piercing damage plus 13 (3d8) radiant damage.

action.name: Slam (Beast Form Only)
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 6 (1d6 + 3) bludgeoning damage plus 13 (3d8) radiant damage.

action.name: Shooting Star
entries: Ranged Spell Attack: +8 to hit, range 60 ft., one target. Hit: 18 (3d8 + 5) radiant damage.

action.name: Invisibility (True Form Only)
entries: The fey guardian magically turns invisible until it attacks, uses Change Shape, or until its concentration ends (as if concentrating on a spell). Any equipment the guardian wears or carries is invisible with it.

action.name: Tutelage
entries: The fey guardian can cast the guidance and minor illusion cantrips and the comprehend languages spell at will without requiring spell components.

bonus.name: Change Shape
entries: The fey guardian magically transforms into a Medium or smaller Beast or Humanoid, or a Small figure of pure light, or back into its true form, which is Fey. Its statistics, other than its size, are the same in each form. Any equipment it is wearing or carrying transforms with it. It reverts to its true form if it dies. Fey Guardian The fairy’s blue hair, glittering wings, and sweet scent contrast sharply with its stern expression.

bonus.name: Linked to Mortals
