entries: The rakshasa makes four Claw or Arcane Bolt attacks. It can replace one attack with a use of Spellcasting. If two Claw attacks hit one creature, the target is cursed. While cursed, the target gains no benefit from finishing a short or long rest. The curse lasts until removed by the remove curse spell or similar magic.

action.name: Claw
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +8 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 12 (2d8 + 3) slashing damage plus 10 (3d6) force damage.

action.name: Arcane Bolt
entries: Ranged Spell Attack: +10 to hit, range 60 ft., one target. Hit: 22 (5d6 + 5) force damage.

action.name: Travel the Planes (1/Day)
entries: The rakshasa magically transports itself to a different plane of existence. This works like the plane shift spell, except the rakshasa can affect only itself and can’t use this action to banish an unwilling creature.

action.name: Spellcasting
entries: The rakshasa casts one of the following spells, requiring no material components and using CHA as the spellcasting ability (spell save DC 18).
At will: detect thoughts, mage hand, minor illusion
3/day each: major image, suggestion
1/day: dominate

bonus.name: Charming Gaze
entries: The rakshasa turns its gaze toward one creature it can see within 60 feet of it. If the target can see the rakshasa, the target must succeed on a DC 18 CHA save or be charmed for 1 minute. A charmed creature can repeat the save at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success. The rakshasa can have up to three creatures charmed in this way at a time.

bonus.name: Illusory Appearance
entries: The rakshasa covers itself and anything it is wearing or carrying with a magical illusion that makes it look like another creature of its general size and Humanoid shape. The illusion ends if the rakshasa takes a bonus action to end it or if the rakshasa dies. The changes wrought by this effect fail to hold up to physical inspection. For example, a creature touching the disguised rakshasa might feel its fur instead of Humanoid flesh. Otherwise, a creature must take an action to visually inspect the illusion and succeed on a DC 25 INT (Investigation) check to discern that the rakshasa is disguised. Remorhaz A bluish-white, segmented insectoid creature rises from the snow, shard-like mandibles snapping. The snow around it vaporizes into steam as the gaps in its carapace glow red with heat.

bonus.name: Arctic Terror
entries: The remorhaz is a terror of cold, northern climes. It blends worm and centipede features with a long, segmented body covered in an icy carapace and rows of small legs the color and shape of icicles. Its segmented carapace vents an extreme, internal heat, melting nearby snow and burning prey and predator alike. A remorhaz unable to vent this heat may try to cool its body by lying in ice or snow, but if no cooling is available eventually its own heat cooks its internal organs.

bonus.name: Indiscriminate Diet
entries: Remorhazes eat almost anything including elk, reindeer, walruses, and polar bears as well as humanoids and even giants. They occasionally feast on the stone and metal ore required to maintain their protective carapaces. A remorhaz can subsist on a diet of only stone and metal ore for a few weeks, but a remorhaz maintaining such a diet risks its carapace becoming too heavy to move or thickened enough to block its vents, preventing it from expelling excess heat.

bonus.name: Debated Origins
entries: Some scholars believe the remorhaz is an odd, arctic off-shoot of the underworld’s purple worm. Remorhazes themselves care little about taxonomy and have devoured many would-be scholars seeking to study them.

trait.name: Heated Body
entries: A creature that touches the remorhaz or hits it with a melee attack while within 5 feet of it takes 7 (2d6) fire damage.

trait.name: Monstrosity Resilience
entries: The remorhaz is resistant to exhaustion and to the frightened condition.

trait.name: Tunneler
entries: The remorhaz can burrow through ice, snow, and permafrost, and it leaves a 5-foot-diameter tunnel in its wake.

action.name: Bite
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +11 to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. Hit: 40 (6d10 + 7) piercing damage plus 10 (3d6) fire damage. If the target is a Medium or smaller creature, it must succeed on a DC 17 DEX save or be swallowed by the remorhaz. A swallowed creature is blinded and restrained, it has total cover against attacks and other effects outside the remorhaz, and it takes 10 (3d6) acid damage and 10 (3d6) fire damage at the start of each of the remorhaz’s turns. If the remorhaz takes 30 damage or more on a single turn from a swallowed creature, the remorhaz must succeed on a DC 19 CON save at the end of that turn or regurgitate all swallowed creatures, which fall prone in a space within 10 feet of the remorhaz. If the remorhaz dies, a swallowed creature is no longer restrained by it and can escape from the corpse by using 15 feet of movement, exiting prone.

bonus.name: Vent Heat (119 HP or Fewer)
entries: As the remorhaz’s life force ebbs, its body generates more heat to protect itself. It can open valves in its carapace to vent this excess heat. Each creature within 5 feet of the remorhaz must succeed on a DC 17 CON save or suffer one level of exhaustion. Robots This metallic construct resembles a beetle with three legs spaced equidistantly around an oval body. A jet of blue flame shoots from its underside, keeping the creature aloft as it zips around. The scent of burning oil fills the air around it. Ancient Relics. Robot drones were created by an advanced civilization to spy on enemy forces, their miniature size and ability to fly enabling them to keep tabs on foes without being detected. Robot wardens were constructed as mostly mindless, obedient warriors to protect the populace. Some such civilizations still exist in lost cities, but most robots have outlived their makers.

bonus.name: Ruins Guardians
entries: Most robots are now scattered throughout wastelands in ancient bunkers, fallen citadels, and even crashed, otherworldly ships, protecting what remains of the people they once served. Many lie dormant until intruders trespass in the ruins or a curious tinkerer ROBOT DRONE FAMILIARS Some robot drones are willing to serve spellcasters, especially spellcasters with an interest in artifice and lost technology. Such drones have the following trait.

bonus.name: Familiar
entries: The robot drone can serve another creature as a familiar, forming a magic, telepathic bond with that willing companion. While the two are bonded, the companion can sense what the drone senses as long as they are within 1 mile of each other. While the drone is within 10 feet of its companion, the companion shares the drone’s Point-Blank Shots trait. At any time and for any reason, the drone can end its service as a familiar, ending the telepathic bond. revives them, sometimes with deadly results. Even when the ruins provide excellent shelter, robots are marked with patches of rust, claw marks from an ancient beast, or they might be missing limbs entirely. In most cases, they can be animated by restoring their internal energies.

bonus.name: Powered by Lightning
entries: Robots rely on electricity to function, and contact with major sources of electricity can disrupt their bodies. Each robot contains a small box at its core that stores the electricity that powers it. Dormant robots can often be restored by charging this box with lightning. Conversely, too much lightning can severely damage a functioning robot.

monster.name: Robot Drone
languages: understands the languages of its creator but can’t speak

trait.name: Construct Nature
entries: The drone doesn’t require air, food, drink, or sleep.

trait.name: Construct Resilience
entries: The drone is immune to poison and psychic damage, and it is immune to exhaustion and the charmed, frightened, paralyzed, petrified, and poisoned conditions.

trait.name: Electrical Malfunction
entries: Whenever the drone takes lightning damage, it must succeed on a DC 11 WIS save or be incapacitated until the end of its next turn.

trait.name: Point-Blank Shots
entries: When the drone makes a ranged attack, it doesn’t have disadvantage on the attack roll from being within 5 feet of a hostile creature, though it may still have disadvantage from other sources.

action.name: Slam
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 7 (1d8 + 3) bludgeoning damage.

action.name: Flame Jet
entries: Ranged Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, range 20/60 ft., one target. Hit: 8 (2d4 + 3) fire damage.

bonus.name: Fueled Escape
entries: The drone takes the Dash or Disengage action, leaving a small cloud of fuel fumes behind it. Each creature within 5 feet must succeed on a DC 13 CON save or be poisoned until the end of its next turn.

trait.name: Construct Nature
entries: The warden doesn’t require air, food, drink, or sleep.

trait.name: Construct Resilience
entries: The warden is immune to poison and psychic damage, and it is immune to exhaustion and the charmed, frightened, paralyzed, petrified, and poisoned conditions. Electrical Malfunction. Whenever the warden takes lightning damage, it must succeed on a DC 11 WIS save or be incapacitated until the end of its next turn.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The robot warden makes two Force Baton attacks.

action.name: Force Baton
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 7 (1d8 + 3) bludgeoning damage plus 7 (2d6) force damage.

action.name: Disrupter Beam (Recharge 5–6)
entries: The warden fires a beam of blue force in a 30-foot line that is 5 feet wide from its chest plate. Each creature in the line must make a DC 13 DEX save. On a failure, a creature takes 14 (4d6) force damage and is stunned until the end of its next turn. On a success, a creature takes half the damage and isn’t stunned. The NO ROBOTS IN MY FANTASY, PLEASE! Many players and GMs don’t like to mix fantasy and science fiction, preferring to keep the two genres separate, and might be turned off by a campaign setting or adventure that incorporates both. The elegant solution to this problem, rather than simply getting rid of the sci-fi elements, is to modify them to make them more palatable to a fantasy audience. A death ray gun might become an archaic wand from a fallen civilization that fires beams of energy, and a robot is simply a golem in a different shell. Even a spaceship can be redefined. Perhaps it is a sky barge of one of the gods of the campaign world or a wondrous gnomish invention powered by elemental forces or tamed dragons. warden can choose not to deal damage with its Disrupter Beam. If it does so, a creature that fails the save is paralyzed for 1 minute instead, and a creature that succeeds suffers no effect. A paralyzed creature can make a DC 13 CON save at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success.

bonus.name: Sensing Pulse
entries: The warden emits a pulse of energy around it, analyzing its surroundings. It automatically detects every creature and trap within 30 feet of it, pinpointing the current location of each. Roc A colossal raptor descends from the sky, its expansive wings blocking out the sun and its thunderous shriek echoing off the mountainsides.

bonus.name: Giant Raptor
entries: This massive bird of prey resembles a crested eagle in general form. The color of a roc’s plumage varies, but a typical roc is a golden brown, with white on the chest, tail, and crest. With wingspans of up to 200 feet and bodies as big as dragons, these mighty aerial hunters can snatch up creatures the size of an elephant in their sharp talons. With larger or more dangerous prey, a roc might fly up into the air then release it, feasting on the creature’s remains in the aftermath of the fall.

bonus.name: High Eyries
entries: Rocs lair on high mountains or coastal cliffs, building massive nests, often of whole trees ripped from the ground. Rocs are solitary creatures, coming together only briefly to mate. Likewise, once the eggs hatch and the young are fully fledged, they fly far away to build their own nests and establish their own hunting grounds.

bonus.name: Fishers
entries: Distant relatives of eagles, rocs frequently hunt marine life like their cousins. A roc might dive at a surfacing whale, snatch the creature out of the water, and carry it back to its seaside nest for a feast. Experienced sailors sailing through known roc territory often chum the waters behind their ships to encourage the rocs to dive for sharks and other marine life instead of deckhands.

trait.name: Heightened Sight
entries: The roc’s Perception is 19 when perceiving by sight.

trait.name: Monstrosity Resilience
entries: The roc is resistant to exhaustion and to the frightened condition.

trait.name: Seabird
entries: The roc can swim up to 60 feet on its turn, but it must start and end its movement either flying or on a solid surface, such as a ship or beach. If it is swimming at the end of its turn, it must succeed on a DC 19 CON save or it immediately begins to sink and suffocate. A suffocating roc must succeed on a DC 19 STR check to fly out of the substance where it is sinking.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The roc makes one Beak attack and two Talon attacks. If both Talon attacks hit one Huge or Gargantuan creature, the target is grappled (escape DC 17). Until this grapple ends, the target is restrained, and the roc can’t use its Talons on another target.

action.name: Beak
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +13 to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. Hit: 27 (4d8 + 9) piercing damage.

action.name: Talon
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +13 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 23 (4d6 + 9) slashing damage, and the target is grappled (escape DC 17) if it is a Large or smaller creature. Until this grapple ends, the target is restrained, and the roc can’t use its Talons on another target.

bonus.name: Territorial Shriek
entries: The roc shrieks a territorial challenge at up to two creatures it can see within 60 feet of it. Each target must succeed on a DC 17 CHA save or be frightened until the end of its next turn. While frightened by this shriek, a creature must take the Dash action on its turn and move away from the roc by the safest available route, unless there is nowhere to move. Roper A spiral of red eyes slowly opens around the top of one of the cavern’s stalagmites. Fleshy tendrils wiggle out of the stalagmite’s sides and whip forward, as a rock-toothed maw opens beneath the eyes.

bonus.name: Hungry Cave Formations
entries: Consummate predators of the deepest caverns, the roper’s conical shape and stony hide resemble a stalactite, stalagmite, or similar cave formation. They have limited control over their coloration and can subtly adjust it to better blend in with the surrounding cavern.

bonus.name: Patient Hunters
entries: Ropers can scale walls and even ceilings for the perfect spot to ambush unsuspecting prey. Patient and needing only a few meals a week to survive, ropers can sit for hours or even days in one location, waiting for prey.

bonus.name: Ropy Tendrils
entries: A roper has a near-endless supply of rope-like tendrils it can extend from its body. When ambushing prey, it uses these tendrils, which contain a weakening poison, to grab, reel in, and devour prey. The roper can also use these tendrils to help it move along cavern walls.

trait.name: Climbing Tendrils
entries: If at least four of the roper’s Grasping Tendrils aren’t grappling creatures, the roper has a climbing speed of 30 feet.

trait.name: False Appearance
entries: While the roper remains motionless, it is indistinguishable from a normal conical or spire-like cave formation, such as a stalagmite or stalactite.

trait.name: Grasping Tendrils
entries: The roper can have up to six Grasping Tendrils at a time. Each Grasping Tendril can be attacked (AC 20; 10 HP; vulnerable to thunder damage; immune to poison and psychic damage). Destroying a Grasping Tendril deals no damage to the roper, which can extrude a replacement tendril on its next turn. A Grasping Tendril can also be broken if a creature takes an action and succeeds on a DC 15 STR check against it.

trait.name: Monstrosity Resilience
entries: The roper is resistant to exhaustion and to the frightened condition.

trait.name: Spider Climb
entries: The roper can climb difficult surfaces, including upside down on ceilings, without needing to make an ability check.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The roper makes one Bite attack and three Grasping Tendril attacks.

action.name: Bite
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +7 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 22 (4d8 + 4) piercing damage.

action.name: Grasping Tendril
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +7 to hit, reach 50 ft., one creature. Hit: 6 (1d4 + 4) bludgeoning damage, and the target is grappled (escape DC 15). Until the grapple ends, the target is restrained and has disadvantage on STR checks and STR saves, and the roper can’t use the same Grasping Tendril on another target. A creature immune to the poisoned condition doesn’t have disadvantage on STR checks and STR saves from the tendril.

bonus.name: Reel
entries: The roper pulls each creature grappled by it up to 25 feet straight toward it.

reaction.name: Snapping Tendril
entries: When a Grasping Tendril that is grappling a creature is destroyed, the roper can quickly pull the remains of that tendril back to it with a whipping snap. Each creature within 10 feet of the roper must succeed on a DC 15 DEX save or be knocked prone. Rust Monster A strange, insectile creature skitters forward on four legs. It waves two large, feathery antennae about in a questing manner.

reaction.name: Rust Eater
entries: As its name implies, this odd creature can rust ferrous metals, and it eats the resulting flakes of metal. The rust monster roams caves and tunnels seeking out such metals. When it finds metal, the creature aggressively rushes to devour the meal. Clever adventurers can often save their weapons or armor by distracting the creature with another source of metal.

reaction.name: Armored Shell
entries: A rust monster is about the size of a large dog. It has armored plates, feathery antennae, and four segmented legs. Its thin, whip-like tail thrashes when it is agitated or catches the scent of metals.

reaction.name: Color Matches Diet
entries: The color of a rust monster’s hide depends on its diet. A rust monster whose diet includes large amounts of mithral would have a silvery sheen, for example, and a rust monster which eats a great deal of copper might have a green or brown tinge.

trait.name: Iron Scent
entries: The rust monster can pinpoint, by scent, the location of ferrous metal within 30 feet of it.

trait.name: Monstrosity Resilience
entries: The rust monster is resistant to exhaustion and to the frightened condition.

trait.name: Rust Metal
entries: Any nonmagical weapon made of metal that hits the rust monster corrodes. After dealing damage, the weapon takes a permanent and cumulative −1 penalty to damage rolls. If its penalty drops to −5, the weapon is destroyed. Nonmagical ammunition made of metal that hits the rust monster is destroyed after dealing damage.

trait.name: Spider Climb
entries: The rust monster can climb difficult surfaces, including upside down on ceilings, without needing to make an ability check.

action.name: Bite
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +3 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 8 (2d6 + 1) piercing damage.

action.name: Antennae
entries: The rust monster corrodes a nonmagical ferrous metal object it can see within 5 feet of it. If the object isn’t being worn or carried, the touch destroys a 1-foot cube of it. If the object is being worn or carried by a creature, the creature can make a DC 11 DEX save to avoid the rust monster’s touch. If the object touched is either metal armor or a metal shield being worn or carried, it takes a permanent and cumulative −1 penalty to the AC it offers. Armor reduced to an AC of 10 or a shield that drops to a +0 bonus is destroyed. If the object touched is a held metal weapon, it rusts as described in the Rust Metal trait.

bonus.name: Iron Dash
entries: The rust monster moves up to half its speed toward a source of ferrous metal it can sense. Sahuagins The scaly humanoid advances, a trident held adeptly in its clawed, webbed hands. Thrashing its heavy tail, the creature opens the mouth of its piscine head to reveal rows of razor-sharp teeth.

bonus.name: Coastal Terrors
entries: Sahuagins are piscine humanoids approximately the same size as humans, but their bodies are covered in gray and green scales with lighter scales along their chests, abdomens, and throats. A large fin extends from the base of each sahuagin’s skull and partway down its spine. They reside in deep ocean waters and in the waters along coastlines. They are almost exclusively carnivorous, consuming whatever creatures they can catch and kill, which sometimes puts them on the wrong side of coastal humanoid communities. Magical Monarchs. Magical ability is rare among sahuagins, and most sahuagins have a deep respect or sometimes awe of magic. This leads many sahuagins to follow magical creatures, such as sea hags, and to elevate the rare magical sahuagins to leadership positions in their communities. These sahuagin monarchs vary widely in their ideals and goals, and they each lead their communities in their own ways. Some sahuagin monarchs prefer their communities barter and trade with coastal humanoids, relying on those humanoids’ forged metals for weapons and tools, but many sahuagin monarchs send their people to prey on coastal humanoids, encouraging those in their community to take by force whatever metal weapons, tools, and shiny baubles they find. This tendency has earned sahuagins the title of “sea devils” in many coastal humanoid settlements.

bonus.name: Shark Affinity
entries: Sahuagins have an innate connection with sharks. The beasts rarely attack sahuagins, often react protectively toward sahuagins in their territory, and instinctively follow telepathic commands from sahuagins. Shark-riding sahuagin captains lead most sahuagin groups that travel away from their settlements. These captains decorate their clothing with the teeth of the various sharks they’ve commanded and wear necklaces sporting the teeth of their favored shark companions from over the years.

trait.name: Blood Frenzy
entries: The sahuagin has advantage on melee attack rolls against any creature that doesn’t have all its HP .

trait.name: Limited Amphibiousness
entries: The sahuagin can breathe air and water, but it needs to be submerged at least once every 4 hours to avoid suffocating.

trait.name: Shark Telepathy
entries: The sahuagin can magically command any shark within 120 feet of it, using a limited telepathy.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The sahuagin makes one Bite attack and one Claws or Trident attack.

action.name: Bite
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +3 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 3 (1d6) piercing damage.

action.name: Claws
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +3 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 5 (2d4) slashing damage.

action.name: Trident
entries: Melee or Ranged Weapon Attack: +3 to hit, reach 5 ft. or range 20/60 ft., one target. Hit: 3 (1d6) piercing damage, or 4 (1d8) piercing damage if used with two hands to make a melee attack. If the target is wielding a weapon or shield, it must succeed on a DC 11 DEX save or drop the weapon or shield (the sahuagin’s choice if the target is wielding both).

reaction.name: Circling Sharks
entries: If the sahuagin ends its turn within 10 feet of at least two other friendly sahuagin or sharks, the sahuagin can snap its teeth at one creature within 15 feet of it that it can see and that can see or hear it. The target must succeed on a DC 11 WIS save or be frightened until the end of its next turn.

trait.name: Blood Frenzy
entries: The sahuagin has advantage on melee attack rolls against any creature that doesn’t have all its HP .

trait.name: Limited Amphibiousness
entries: The sahuagin can breathe air and water, but it needs to be submerged at least once every 4 hours to avoid suffocating.

trait.name: Shark Telepathy
entries: The sahuagin can magically command any shark within 120 feet of it, using a limited telepathy.

trait.name: Shark-Tooth Adornments
entries: A creature that touches the sahuagin captain or hits it with a melee attack while within 5 feet of it takes 3 (1d6) piercing damage.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The sahuagin captain makes one Bite attack and two Claw or Trident attacks.

action.name: Bite
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +4 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 5 (1d6 + 2) piercing damage.

action.name: Claw
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +4 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 7 (2d4 + 2) slashing damage.

action.name: Trident
entries: Melee or Weapon Attack: +4 to hit, reach 5 ft. or range 20/60 ft., one target. Hit: 5 (1d6 + 2) piercing damage, or 6 (1d8 + 2) piercing damage if used with two hands to make a melee attack. If the target is wielding a weapon or shield, it must succeed on a DC 12 DEX save or drop the weapon or shield (the captain’s choice if the target is wielding both).

action.name: Crisscross Attack (Recharge 4–6)
entries: The captain commands up to two friendly creatures it can see, which can include the captain, within 15 feet of a hostile target to each use a reaction to charge in a straight line up to its speed through the target, moving through the target’s space regardless of the target’s size and without provoking opportunity attacks. The target must make a DC 12 DEX save, taking 21 (6d6) slashing damage on a failed save, or half as much damage on a successful one.

bonus.name: Circle Prey
entries: Up to three friendly creatures the captain can see within 30 feet of the captain can each use a reaction to move up to its speed toward a target of the captain’s choice. This movement doesn’t provoke opportunity attacks.

trait.name: Blood Frenzy
entries: The sahuagin has advantage on melee attack rolls against any creature that doesn’t have all its HP .

trait.name: Limited Amphibiousness
entries: The sahuagin can breathe air and water, but it needs to be submerged at least once every 4 hours to avoid suffocating.

trait.name: Shark Telepathy
entries: The sahuagin can magically command any shark within 120 feet of it, using a limited telepathy.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The salamander makes one Trident attack and one Tail attack, or it makes three Fire Bolt attacks. It can replace one Trident attack with a Tail attack.

action.name: Trident
entries: Melee or Ranged Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft. or range 20/60 ft., one target. Hit: 10 (2d6 + 3) piercing damage, or 12 (2d8 + 3) piercing damage if used with two hands to make a melee attack, plus 7 (2d6) fire damage. If the target is wielding a weapon or shield, it must succeed on a DC 14 DEX save or drop the weapon or shield (the salamander’s choice if the target is wielding both).

action.name: Tail
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 10 ft., one target. Hit: 10 (2d6 + 3) bludgeoning damage plus 7 (2d6) fire damage, and the target is grappled (escape DC 14). Until this grapple ends, the target is restrained, the salamander can automatically hit the target with its tail, and the salamander can’t make tail attacks against other targets.

action.name: Fire Bolt
entries: Ranged Spell Attack: +5 to hit, ranged 60 ft., one target. Hit: 12 (3d6 + 2) fire damage.

reaction.name: Text
entries: Protect the Crown! When the monarch is attacked by a creature it can see within 30 feet of it, the monarch can command a friendly shark or sahuagin within 5 feet of it to intercept the attack. That shark or sahuagin becomes the new target of the attack. MULTI-ARMED MONARCHS Some powerful sahuagin monarchs develop additional arms and claws as they age. Though these claws can eventually become strong enough to wield weapons, most multi-armed monarchs use the claws in conjunction with their more powerful trident and bite attacks. Such monarchs have the following bonus action:

reaction.name: Rending Claw
entries: The sahuagin rakes one creature within 5 feet of it with its extra claws. The target must succeed on a DC 13 STR save or suffer a bleeding wound. A creature with a bleeding wound loses 10 (4d4) HP at the start of each of its turns. Any creature can take an action to stanch the wound with a successful DC 12 WIS (Medicine) check. The wound also closes if the target receives magical healing.

bonus.name: Tighten Grip
entries: The salamander tightens its grip around one creature it is grappling. Until the start of the salamander’s next turn, the target has disadvantage on any check made to escape the grapple, and other than a successful check to escape the grapple, the target can’t be moved, knocked prone, or otherwise removed from the salamander’s grasp without the salamander’s permission. Salamander This red-scaled snake has the torso of a reptilian humanoid. Waves of heat roll off it as it hisses a warning and jabs its red-hot trident forward.

bonus.name: Planar Serpents
entries: Salamanders are snake-like inhabitants of the Plane of Fire. They have serpentine lower bodies with humanoid torsos and arms. Their bodies are covered in red scales that ripple and flicker with heat and flames, and their eyes glow like coals in their reptilian faces. They bathe in pools of lava and can’t tolerate climates cooler than tropical when on the Material Plane.

bonus.name: Spreading Heat
entries: The heat generated by a salamander’s body burns those in contact with it. This extends to the salamander’s weapons, which are typically of a metal that can channel the heat into the salamander’s enemies. This heat makes salamanders particularly dangerous on the Material Plane, especially when outside of the volcanic areas they prefer, as merely slithering through a forest can start a forest fire.

bonus.name: Strength Above All
entries: Salamanders value strength and skill at arms. The strongest and most martially skilled of them typically rule the rest in each of their communities. This view often leads them to have little respect or regard for mortals, who they typically view as smaller and weaker creatures. They respect mortals who display great martial prowess or strength, and they often allow such mortals safe passage into their communities, task such mortals with needs they might otherwise be unable to fulfill, or permit such mortals to make requests for aid from them.

monster.name: Salamander
resistNoteFromTraits: bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing damage from

trait.name: Heated Body
entries: A creature that touches the salamander or hits it with a melee attack while within 5 feet of it takes 3 (1d6) fire damage.

trait.name: Heated Weapons
entries: When the salamander hits with a metal melee weapon, the weapon deals an extra 7 (2d6) fire damage (included in the attack). Satarre Light glints off the glossy flesh of the wiry horror as it extends its clawed hands in a fighter’s stance.

trait.name: World Enders
entries: The satarre call themselves the “devourers” or “those who end the world” due to their veneration of dark gods who seek to bring about an apocalypse. Most of them strive to destroy the worlds of mortals and bring about the end of all things.

trait.name: Planar Travelers
entries: The satarre know many worlds and are comfortable speaking with planar beings, as their people have long been those who turn grand and maleficent visions into actionable plans.

trait.name: Satarre Destroyers
entries: Warriors of the satarre, destroyers strike down the satarres' enemies across the planes and lead the undead legions created by their spellcasters.

trait.name: Void Strength
entries: The satarre is resistant to the blinded, charmed, deafened, frightened, stunned, and unconscious conditions.

trait.name: Void Weapons
entries: The satarre’s weapon attacks are magical. When the satarre hits with any weapon, the weapon deals an extra 1d6 necrotic damage (included in the attack).

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The satarre makes two Claw or Spear attacks.

action.name: Claw
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +4 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 5 (1d6 + 2) piercing damage plus 3 (1d6) necrotic damage.

action.name: Spear
entries: Melee or Ranged Weapon Attack: +4 to hit, reach 5 ft. or range 20/60 ft., one target. Hit: 5 (1d6 + 2) piercing damage plus 3 (1d6) necrotic damage.

trait.name: Void Strength
entries: As the satarre.

trait.name: Void Weapons
entries: As the satarre, except the destroyer's weapon deals an extra 2d6 necrotic damage.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The satarre destroyer makes one Claw attack and one Greataxe attack, or it makes two Necrotic Bolt attacks.

action.name: Claw
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 6 (1d6 + 3) slashing damage plus 7 (2d6) necrotic damage.

action.name: Greataxe
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 9 (1d12 + 3) slashing damage plus 7 (2d6) necrotic damage. If the target is a Medium or smaller creature, it must succeed on a DC 13 STR save or be knocked prone.

action.name: Necrotic Bolt
entries: Ranged Spell Attack: +4 to hit, range 60 ft., one target. Hit: 12 (3d6 + 2) necrotic damage.

bonus.name: Coordinate Strikes
entries: One friendly satarre or Undead of the destroyer’s choice within 30 feet of the destroyer has advantage on the next attack roll it makes against a creature the destroyer attacked this round. Satyr The horned goat-man smiles and winks slyly as he offers a mug sloshing with ale.

bonus.name: Goat Fey
entries: These wild, forest-dwelling fey resemble hirsute humanoids from the waist up, with wooly legs and cloven hooves comprising their lower halves. Curling, ram- like horns grow from their heads.

bonus.name: Revelries
entries: Satyrs are indulgent creatures, hungering for all types of sensory pleasures and seeking them whenever possible. They prefer to indulge in company, and they have little qualms about coercing others to share in the revelry, with little thought to possible consequences. Likewise, they happily participate in any ongoing festivities, whether or not they are invited. Satyrs often carry magical pipes, and they use enchanted music to sway people into joining their revelries.

bonus.name: Forest Protectors
entries: Despite their carefree, hedonistic natures, satyrs are devoted defenders of their forest homelands, and they quickly rise to the defense of the forest against those that would despoil or destroy it. In such instances, their pan pipes can produce chords that strike fear into the satyr’s enemies.

trait.name: Fey Resilience
entries: The satyr is resistant to the charmed and unconscious conditions.

trait.name: Magic Resistance
entries: The satyr has advantage on saves against spells and other magical effects.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The selang makes two Dagger or Poison Bolt attacks.

action.name: Dagger
entries: Melee or Ranged Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft. or range 20/60 ft., one target. Hit: 6 (1d4 + 4) piercing damage plus 10 (3d6) poison damage.

action.name: Poison Bolt
entries: Ranged Spell Attack: +6 to hit, range 60 ft., one target. Hit: 14 (3d6 + 4) poison damage.

bonus.name: Alien Piping
entries: The selang plays one of the following ear-bending melodies on alien pipes. One creature the selang can see within 60 feet of it is the only creature that can hear the melody. The target must succeed on a DC 14 WIS save or be affected by the chosen melody.
• Capering. On the target’s next turn, it must use all of its

movement dancing without leaving its space, and it has disadvantage on attack rolls and DEX saves. Selangs This grinning man would be quite handsome, if not for his glowing red eyes, insectoid legs, and antennae.

bonus.name: Dark Satyrs
entries: The selangs or dark satyrs are twisted and vicious fauns who have abandoned nature worship and instead venerate ancient gods of deep and malign intelligence. Selangs seek to help those malevolent gods enter the mortal world by opening dark portals and bridging a path to realms beyond mortal understanding.

bonus.name: Battle Song and Laughter
entries: Selangs relish battle, pain, and torture. They find violence thrilling and bloodshed exciting, and they often laugh, sing, and boast shamelessly during combat. Although they are the diplomats and spokesmen of the old gods, their manic speech and alien logic can sometimes be hard to follow. They are most comfortable with the slithering tones of Void Speech.

bonus.name: Blasphemous Music
entries: Selang cults and settlements are often found at the sites sacred to the dark gods, making hypnotic and alien harmonies. They are rarely the strongest soldiers, instead encouraging others of martial mien to fill the ranks, while the dark satyrs use their magic and poison against their foes.

bonus.name: Void Piping
entries: Some selang take their devotion to their dark gods to an even deeper level, exposing themselves more fully to the corrupting power of the Void, which reshapes the selang. The most notable change this exposure causes in selangs is the growth of fleshy tubes of differing lengths on the side of the selang’s neck. These selangs channel the power of the Void through songs hummed and piped through these tubes rather than the manufactured instruments of other selangs. Void pipers, as they are known among selangs, feel much less like individuals and believe they are living conduits of the Void’s power and their gods’ wills. Channeling the Void brings them great joy, while simultaneously damaging the minds of creatures around them not accustomed to the Void.

monster.name: Selang
resistNoteFromTraits: bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing damage from

trait.name: Text
entries: • Captivating. The target is charmed until the end of
its next turn, humming along to the selang’s pleasant melody.
• Disorienting. The target is incapacitated until the end of
its next turn, and when it moves, it moves in a random direction.
• Drowsy. The target falls unconscious and dreams of evil
entities and sinister portals for 10 minutes. The creature wakes up if it takes damage or if another creature takes an action to shake it awake.
• Haunting. The target is frightened until the end of its next
turn, and on its turn, it must take the Dash action and move away from the selang by the safest available route, unless there is nowhere to move.

monster.name: Void Piper
resistNoteFromTraits: bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing damage from

trait.name: Fey Resilience
entries: The void piper is resistant to the charmed and unconscious conditions.

trait.name: Void-Tainted Weapons
entries: The void piper’s weapons are coated with a magical poison tainted by the Void. When the void piper hits with any weapon, the weapon deals an extra 3d8 necrotic damage (included in the attack).

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The selkie makes one Bite attack and two Tail attacks, or it makes three Spear attacks.

action.name: Bite (Seal Form Only)
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 10 (2d6 +3) piercing damage.

action.name: Spear (Fey Form Only)
entries: Melee or Ranged Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft. or range 20/60 ft., one target. Hit: 7 (1d6 + 4) piercing damage, or 8 (1d8 + 4) piercing damage if used with two hands to make a melee attack.

action.name: Tail (Seal Form Only)
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 8 (1d8 + 4) bludgeoning damage.

bonus.name: Change Shape
entries: The selkie magically transforms into a Medium seal or back into its true form, which is Fey. Its statistics, other than its AC, size, and speed, are the same in each form. Any equipment it is wearing or carrying transforms with it. It reverts to its true form if it dies. If the selkie is in its true form, it can’t use this bonus action if it isn’t wearing or carrying its bound sealskin.

bonus.name: Charming Escape
entries: The selkie can take the Dash or Disengage action, performing a chuckle, twirl, or wink as it does. Each creature within 5 feet must succeed on a DC 13 CHA save or be charmed until the end of its next turn. Selkie A young man dances on the shore, graceful upon the sand. Suddenly he stops and reaches for a nearby cloak. As it swirls around his shoulders, he disappears. In his place, a seal slips beneath the ocean waves.

bonus.name: Transforming Fey
entries: Selkies are fey creatures who transform into seals using magical sealskins. Their humanoid forms are beautiful, with webbed hands and feet. If deprived of its sealskin, a selkie can’t change its shape and will do all it can to retrieve the skin. Selkies vary in temperament; some are kind, while others are malicious tricksters.

bonus.name: Migratory Creatures
entries: Selkies live on secluded islands but travel to coastlands in summer to trade with coastal enclaves, sometimes even wooing or marrying among fisherfolk. Selkies rarely remain, for the sea, and its freedom, calls to them each high tide.

bonus.name: Enchanted Skins
entries: Selkies believe that long ago, an archfey of seals gave them their magical sealskins. Stealing a selkie’s sealskin is considered the gravest of crimes, and they will band together to retrieve a stolen sealskin, often with violent consequences. Some selkies have learned to disguise their skins as items of clothing, especially disheveled or mundane clothing, such as belts, hats, vests, or cloaks to avoid attracting the attention of thieves. An individual selkie’s skin works only for them, becoming nonmagical when it dies, and can’t be used by anyone else, even other selkies.

bonus.name: Allied Sea Tribes
entries: Though considerably rare, legends tell of selkie tribes that use the skins of other sea animals to change shape. Such selkies use the skins of sharks, giant sea horses, or even orcas, and can assume those forms instead of a seal form.

ac.note: 12 in fey form, 14 (natural armor) in seal form

trait.name: Fey Resilience
entries: The selkie is resistant to the charmed and unconscious conditions.

trait.name: Hold Breath
entries: The selkie can hold its breath for 30 minutes. Shadow A man’s shadow flickers in the firelight of the streetlamp. The man moves further down the street, but his shadow remains, steadily becoming more solid as it pulls away from the cobblestones beneath the lamp.

trait.name: Covetousness Incarnate
entries: A shadow arises in the wake of the death of a humanoid with an overwhelmingly covetous nature. Cowardice, greed, gluttony, jealousy, miserliness, and similar flaws cause corruption that taints the spirit, making it linger as a shadow upon the humanoid’s death. In undeath, covetousness turns into a hunger for the strength and vibrance of the living. Shadows appear much as their namesakes, but their deathly natures manifest as monstrous, exaggerated versions of the humanoid form, such as overlong limbs, imposing silhouettes, fanged maws, and eyes that glow a dim red.

trait.name: Hidden Danger
entries: Shadows lurk in dark, abandoned places, such as ruined cities, abandoned graveyards, or dank dungeons, clinging to floors, walls, and ceilings until living creatures foolishly or unknowingly enter their domains. Once a potential victim is in sight, a shadow hides as part of the victim’s mundane shadow, then strikes when the victim is most vulnerable. A humanoid slain by a shadow becomes a shadow itself.

monster.name: Shadow
languages: understands the languages it knew in life but can’t speak

trait.name: Amorphous
entries: The shadow can move through a space as narrow as 1 inch wide without squeezing.

trait.name: Spider Climb
entries: The shadow can climb difficult surfaces, including upside down on ceilings, without needing to make an ability check.

trait.name: Sunlight Weakness
entries: While in sunlight, the shadow has disadvantage on attack rolls, ability checks, and saves.

trait.name: Undead Nature
entries: The shadow doesn’t require air, food, drink, or sleep.

trait.name: Undead Resilience
entries: The shadow is immune to poison damage, to exhaustion, and to the poisoned condition.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The shambling mound makes two Slam attacks. If both attacks hit a Medium or smaller target, the target is grappled (escape DC 14).

action.name: Slam
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +7 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 13 (2d8 + 4) bludgeoning damage plus 7 (2d6) poison damage.

bonus.name: Engulf
entries: The shambling mound engulfs a Medium or smaller creature grappled by it. In addition to being grappled, the engulfed target is blinded, restrained, and unable to breathe, and it must succeed on a DC 14 CON save at the start of each of the mound’s turns or take 4 (1d8) bludgeoning damage and 3 (1d6) poison damage. If the mound moves, the engulfed target moves with it. The mound can have only one creature engulfed at a time.

monster.name: Shambling Mound
senses: keensense 60 ft. (can’t sense beyond this radius)

trait.name: Lightning Absorption
entries: Whenever the shambling mound is subjected to lightning damage, it takes no damage and regains HP equal to the lightning damage dealt.

trait.name: Plant Resilience
entries: The shambling mound is resistant to exhaustion and to the paralyzed, petrified, and unconscious conditions.

trait.name: Rotting Vegetation
entries: The shambling mound emits the smell of rotting vegetation. A creature that starts its turn within 10 feet of the shambling mound must succeed on a DC 14 CON save or be poisoned until the start of its next turn. Shield Guardian A towering mechanical humanoid with one arm shaped like a shield and a body covered in glowing runes strides forward.

trait.name: Crafted Protectors
entries: The quintessential protective construct, shield guardians are crafted and enchanted to defend their wards at all costs. Shield guardians vary in shape and style, based on the tastes of their makers, but each shield guardian displays glowing runes and has at least one shield-like limb. They can store the magical energy of spellcasters, and spellcasters must be involved in the creation of each shield guardian. A shield guardian created without the assistance of a spellcaster has the statistics of a suit of animated armor.

trait.name: Amulet Bound
entries: When a shield guardian is constructed, its maker also crafts an amulet that is magically linked to the construct. The shield guardian is bound to protect and follow the orders of the wearer of the amulet. This enables control of a shield guardian to be transferred, or stolen, as anyone bearing the amulet can issue commands to the construct and gain its protection. Some spellcasters craft shield guardians specifically to sell or gift as personal defenders, often to a lover, dear friend, or honored liege.

monster.name: Shield Guardian
languages: understands the languages of its creator and the languages of the wearer of its amulet but can’t speak

trait.name: Bound
entries: The shield guardian is magically bound to an amulet. As long as the guardian and its amulet are on the same plane of existence, the guardian knows the distance and direction to the amulet’s wearer, and the amulet’s wearer can telepathically call the guardian, teleporting the guardian to an unoccupied space within 5 feet of the wearer after 1 minute. In addition, if the guardian is within 60 feet of the amulet’s wearer, half of any damage the wearer takes (rounded up) is taken by the guardian instead.

trait.name: Construct Nature
entries: The shield guardian doesn’t require air, food, drink, or sleep.

trait.name: Construct Resilience
entries: The shield guardian is immune to poison and psychic damage, and it is immune to exhaustion and the charmed, frightened, paralyzed, petrified, and poisoned conditions.

trait.name: Regeneration
entries: The shield guardian regains 10 HP at the start of its turn if it has at least 1 HP .

trait.name: Spell Storing
entries: A spellcaster who wears the shield guardian’s amulet can cause the guardian to store one spell of 4th level or lower. To do so, the wearer must cast the spell on the guardian. The spell has no effect but is stored within the guardian. When commanded to do so by the wearer or when a situation arises that was predefined by the spellcaster, the guardian casts the stored spell, using the appropriate action required by the spell, with any parameters set by the original caster, requiring no components. When the spell is cast or a new spell is stored, any previously stored spell is lost.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The shield guardian makes two Runed Fist attacks, or it makes three Rune Bolt attacks.

action.name: Runed Fist
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +7 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 13 (2d8 + 4) bludgeoning damage plus 14 (4d8) force damage.

action.name: Rune Bolt
entries: Ranged Spell Attack: +5 to hit, range 60 ft., one target. Hit: 16 (4d6 + 2) force damage.

bonus.name: Protective Step
entries: The guardian teleports up to 60 feet to an unoccupied space it can see within 5 feet of the amulet’s wearer.

reaction.name: Shield
entries: When a creature the guardian can see makes an attack against the amulet’s wearer, the guardian grants a +2 bonus to the wearer’s AC if the guardian is within 5 feet of the wearer. Skeletons Half hidden in darkness, skeletal bones clad in rusted armor and threadbare clothing lurch forward, the creature’s bony fingers clutched around the rotting grip of a rusted sword.

reaction.name: Undead Servitor
entries: Skeletons are fleshless undead that have been reconstructed and animated by fell magic. Soulless, emotionless, and driven by the whims of their master, the skeleton is an unshakable guardian. Skeletons can be found in desecrated temples, graveyards, and cemeteries where they can watch, completely motionless, for eons. New and masterful necromancers use skeletons as stalwart guards and servants, though they understand such creatures have no loyalty beyond the magic that controls them.

reaction.name: Dark Intelligence
entries: While lacking humanoid intelligence, skeletons possess a cleverness that extends beyond a mere beast. They remember how to wield weapons and wear armor, and they collect and use weapons or shields taken from the recent dead. Skeletons look upon the living with eyeless sockets and unsettling patience, as their instincts judge distances, tactics, and methods to separate a potential target’s flesh from their bones.

reaction.name: Moldering Bodies
entries: Though dried strands of tendon or wisps of stubborn hair may cling to a skeleton, magic alone is responsible for its movement. Piercing weapons and arrows may skip from the skeleton’s hardened bone, but heavy blows shatter them.

monster.name: Minotaur Skeleton
languages: understands the languages it knew in life but can’t speak

trait.name: Charge
entries: If the minotaur skeleton moves at least 10 feet straight toward a target and then hits it with a Gore attack on the same turn, the target takes an extra 7 (2d6) piercing damage. If the target is a creature, it must succeed on a DC 14 STR save or be pushed up to 10 feet away from the skeleton and knocked prone.

trait.name: False Appearance
entries: While the minotaur skeleton remains motionless and prone, it is indistinguishable from an inanimate minotaur skeleton. Immune Undead Resilience Senses darkvision 60 ft. Languages — STR DEX CON INT WIS CHA +4 +1 +2 −4 +1 −3 False Appearance. While the war horse skeleton remains motionless and prone, it is indistinguishable from an inanimate war horse skeleton.

trait.name: Undead Nature
entries: The skeleton doesn’t require air, food, drink, or sleep.

trait.name: Undead Resilience
entries: The skeleton is immune to poison damage, to exhaustion, and to the poisoned condition.

action.name: Greataxe
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 17 (2d12 + 4) slashing damage.

action.name: Gore
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 11 (2d6 + 4) piercing damage.

bonus.name: Counterattack (Recharge 6)
entries: When a creature the war horse skeleton can see hits it with a melee attack while within 5 feet of it, the skeleton can make one Hooves attack against the attacker.

bonus.name: Labyrinthine Memories
entries: The minotaur skeleton has advantage on checks and saves to avoid becoming lost or to recall a path it has previously traveled.

bonus.name: Undead Nature
entries: The skeleton doesn’t require air, food, drink, or sleep.

bonus.name: Undead Resilience
entries: The skeleton is immune to poison damage, to exhaustion, and to the poisoned condition.

reaction.name: Counterattack (Recharge 6)
entries: When a creature the minotaur skeleton can see hits it with a melee attack while within 5 feet of it, the skeleton can make one Gore attack against the attacker.

monster.name: Skeleton
languages: understands the languages it knew in life but can’t speak

entries: While the skeleton remains motionless and prone, it is indistinguishable from an inanimate Humanoid skeleton.

trait.name: Undead Nature
entries: The skeleton doesn’t require air, food, drink, or sleep.

trait.name: Undead Resilience

entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +4 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 5 (1d6 + 2) piercing damage.

action.name: Shortbow
entries: Ranged Weapon Attack: +4 to hit, range 80/320 ft., one target. Hit: 5 (1d6 + 2) piercing damage.

reaction.name: Text
entries: Counterattack (Recharge 6). When a creature the skeleton can see hits it with an attack, the skeleton can make one Shortsword or Shortbow attack against the attacker.

trait.name: False Appearance

trait.name: Undead Resilience

monster.name: Specter
languages: understands the languages it knew in life but can’t speak

trait.name: Incorporeal Movement
entries: The specter can move through other creatures and objects as if they were difficult terrain. It takes 5 (1d10) force damage if it ends its turn inside an object. Sunlight Sensitivity. While in sunlight, the specter has disadvantage on attack rolls, and its Perception is 5 when perceiving by sight.

trait.name: Undead Nature
entries: The specter doesn’t require air, food, drink, or sleep.

trait.name: Undead Resilience
entries: The specter is immune to poison damage, to exhaustion, and to the poisoned condition.

action.name: Life Drain
entries: Melee Spell Attack: +4 to hit, reach 5 ft., one creature. Hit: 12 (3d6 + 2) necrotic damage. The target must succeed on a DC 12 CON save or its HP maximum is reduced by an amount equal to the damage taken. This reduction lasts until the target finishes a long rest. The target dies if this effect reduces its HP maximum to 0.

reaction.name: Retributive Shriek
entries: When the specter takes damage from a creature it can see within 30 feet of it, the specter can shriek with the fury of its tormented existence. That creature must succeed on a DC 12 WIS save or be frightened until the end of its next turn. Sphinxes A creature reclines on the ledge above. Great feathered wings rest folded atop a lion’s body that bears a regal humanoid head.

reaction.name: Agents of the Divine
entries: Sphinxes are powerful, enigmatic, solitary creatures subject to divine or planar forces. They are direct agents of powerful extraplanar beings, typically deities, demigods, or similar entities. The role of a sphinx is dependent upon the being it serves and the mission on which it is sent to the Material Plane. A sphinx might be sent as punishment, attacking and killing the folk of a region or city, or it might be sent as a guardian over something important to the extraplanar entity: a powerful magical site, great stores of lost knowledge, or even a prison filled with powerful and dangerous beings. A sphinx might also be tasked with overseeing an important trial or test that a mortal is undertaking for the sphinx’s superior, ensuring the trial isn’t stopped, altered, or otherwise disrupted.

reaction.name: Lovers of Riddles
entries: Sphinxes are most often tasked with indefinitely guarding an area or indefinitely overseeing a trial or test that occurs regularly every few years or even every few hundred years. Sphinxes typically enjoy wordplay and riddles, and sphinxes tasked with such long assignments often entertain themselves in the interim between visitors by coming up with complex riddles. They challenge visitors to answer these riddles, and each sphinx reacts differently to riddles being answered correctly or incorrectly. Creatures that are particularly clever with riddles or wordplay might be allowed to pass through a guarded area they otherwise might not be allowed to enter, while those who fail to answer even the simplest riddle might face the wrath of an agitated sphinx.

reaction.name: Variety of Sphinxes
entries: Sphinxes come in a variety of styles, often based on the entity that created or commands the sphinx, but the two most common types of sphinxes are androsphinxes and gynosphinxes. Androsphinxes typically have heads that resemble masculine humanoids, complete with well-groomed beards. They tend to be pedantic and overly ceremonial, and they possess a magical roar that they employ most often against those who do not acknowledge their power or authority. Gynosphinxes typically have heads that resemble feminine humanoids but with more feline features, such as slitted eyes or a mouth full of sharp teeth. They tend to be quick-witted and possess considerable mental might, which they use to reroute or disorient unwanted visitors. A SPHINX’S LAIR A sphinx’s lair is typically an ancient ruin, abandoned library, long-forgotten treasury, deserted temple, or similar location once inhabited by humanoids or sacred to some extraplanar entity. These lairs are most often in deserts, but a sphinx lair can be anywhere humanoids once dwelled. LAIR ACTIONS On initiative count 20 (losing initiative ties), the sphinx can take a lair action to cause one of the following effects; the sphinx can’t use the same effect two rounds in a row:
• Balance Time. Time around the sphinx becomes

balanced. Each creature within 30 feet of the sphinx affected by a spell or other effect that lasts for more than 1 round has that effect’s duration reduced to 1 round. An unwilling creature must succeed on a DC 17 WIS save to avoid the reduction.
• Enshroud. A 20-foot-radius sphere of magical

darkness appears centered on a point the sphinx can see within 120 feet of it. While inside the sphere, the sphinx makes no noise unless it chooses to do so. The magical darkness lasts until initiative count 20 on the next round.
• Sit and Listen. Up to three creatures the sphinx can

see within 120 feet of it are pulled to the ground. Each target must succeed on a DC 17 STR save or be knocked prone and restrained until the end of its next turn. If a target fails the save while flying, it is immediately grounded, taking falling damage as normal, is knocked prone, and is restrained until the end of its next turn.
• Transpose. Up to two creatures the sphinx can see

within 120 feet of it switch places, each teleporting into the space of the other. The sphinx can’t target itself with this lair action. REGIONAL EFFECTS The region containing a sphinx’s lair is altered by its presence, which creates one or more of the following effects:
• Defensive Storms. Dust storms are more frequent
within 5 miles of the sphinx’s lair. Creatures intentionally seeking the lair encounter such storms every 1d4 hours of travel in the area.
• Guarded Secrets. A creature that finishes a short or
long rest within 1 mile of the sphinx’s lair must succeed on a DC 13 WIS save or forget any interaction it had with the sphinx that the sphinx doesn’t want it to remember.
• Riddle Speak. For every 24 hours a creature spends
within 2 miles of a sphinx’s lair, it must succeed on a DC 13 CHA save or speak only in riddles for 8 hours.
• Truth Manifest. Within 3 miles of the

sphinx’s lair, creatures have disadvantage on CHA (Deception) checks, and a creature that knowingly tells a lie suffers great pain for 1 minute. If a creature knowingly tells another lie while suffering this pain, it takes 2 (1d4) psychic damage. The sphinx is aware of the distance, direction, and identity of a creature that takes this damage. If the sphinx dies, these effects fade in 1d10 days.

reaction.name: Arcane Bolt
entries: Ranged Spell Attack: +12 to hit, range 120 ft., one target. Hit: 24 (4d8 + 6) force damage.

reaction.name: Spellcasting
entries: The sphinx casts one of the following spells, requiring no material components and using WIS as the spellcasting ability (spell save DC 18).
At will: command, detect evil and good, thaumaturgy
3/day each: dispel magic, restoration, tongues, zone of truth
2/day each: freedom of movement, greater restoration
1/day: heal

bonus.name: Roar
entries: The sphinx emits one of the following magical roars. Each roar is audible up to 300 feet away. When it roars, the sphinx chooses up to three creatures it can see within 120 feet of it to be affected by the roar. A creature that fails a save and is affected by an ongoing effect can repeat the save at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success.
• Rumbling Roar. This deep roar rumbles through each
target’s body, rattling it to its bones. Each target must succeed on a DC 18 CON save or deal only half damage with weapon attacks that use STR or DEX for 1 minute.

trait.name: Inscrutable
entries: The sphinx is immune to any effect that would sense its emotions or read its thoughts, as well as any divination spell that it refuses. WIS (Insight) checks made to ascertain the sphinx’s intentions or sincerity have disadvantage.

trait.name: Magical Claws
entries: The sphinx’s Claw attacks are magical. When the sphinx hits with a Claw attack, the Claw deals an extra 2d8 force damage (included in the attack).

trait.name: Monstrosity Resilience
entries: The sphinx is resistant to exhaustion and to the frightened condition.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The sphinx makes four Claw or Arcane Bolt attacks. It can replace one attack with a use of Spellcasting.

action.name: Claw
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +12 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 17 (2d10 + 6) slashing damage plus 9 (2d8) force damage.
• Terrifying Roar. This shrill roar tugs at

the self-preservation instincts within creatures. Each target must succeed on a DC 18 WIS save or be frightened for 1 minute. While frightened, a creature’s speed is 0. If a creature fails the save by 5 or more, it is also paralyzed while frightened.
• Thunderous Roar. This booming roar
slams against the chosen creatures. Each target must succeed on a DC 18 STR save or be pushed up to 15 feet away from the sphinx, knocked prone, and deafened for 1 minute.

legendary.name: Text
entries: The sphinx can take 3 legendary actions, choosing from the options below. Only one legendary action option can be used at a time and only at the end of another creature’s turn. The sphinx regains spent legendary actions at the start of its turn.

legendary.name: Teleport
entries: The sphinx magically teleports, along with any equipment it is wearing or carrying, up to 120 feet to an unoccupied space it can see.

legendary.name: Cast a Spell (Costs 2 Actions)
entries: The sphinx uses Spellcasting.

legendary.name: Lacerating Swipe (Costs 2 Actions)
entries: The sphinx rears up on its hind legs, wings fluttering, and swipes in a wide arc with both its forepaws. Each creature in a 15-foot cone must succeed on a DC 18 DEX save or take 11 (2d10) slashing damage and suffer a bleeding wound. A creature with a bleeding wound loses 5 (1d10) HP at the start of each of its turns. Any creature can take an action to stanch the wound with a successful DC 18 WIS (Medicine) check. The wound also closes if the bleeding creature receives magical healing.

monster.name: Gynosphinx
resistNoteFromTraits: bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing damage from

trait.name: Inscrutable
entries: The sphinx is immune to any effect that would sense its emotions or read its thoughts, as well as any divination spell that it refuses. WIS (Insight) checks made to ascertain the sphinx’s intentions or sincerity have disadvantage. SPHINX RIDDLES The following riddles are examples of the types of riddles a sphinx might ask and can be used as inspiration for your own riddles. The answers to the riddles are included at the end. RIDDLE 1 A shining smile within the dark And yet a disc with graceful arc From ‘neath the ground I do embark A cold white fire without a spark By lovers loved By wolves respected The oceans of the world affected I change my face with every night From yawning dark to shining light What am I? RIDDLE 2 Where you have two, I’ve many more That keep me moving ‘cross the floor Until I cease to move at all And spin myself a shroud The life I’ve known I leave behind My form and function redesigned Tearing from within my caul I fly away, now proud What am I? RIDDLE 3 I swallow all I hold the bones Remembrances Spelled out with stones On hallowed ground Where grass doth grow Yet worms do feast In soil below What am I?

trait.name: Riddle Answers
entries: 1: moon; 2: caterpillar or butterfly; 3: graveyard or cemetery Monstrosity Resilience. The sphinx is resistant to exhaustion and to the frightened condition.

trait.name: Psychic Claws
entries: The sphinx’s Claw attacks are magical. When the sphinx hits with a Claw attack, the Claw deals an extra 3d6 psychic damage (included in the attack).

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The sphinx makes three Claw attacks, or it makes four Psychic Bolt attacks. It can replace one attack with a use of Spellcasting.

action.name: Claw
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +8 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 15 (2d10 + 4) slashing damage plus 10 (3d6) psychic damage.

action.name: Psychic Bolt
entries: Ranged Spell Attack: +9 to hit, range 120 ft., one target. Hit: 19 (4d6 + 5) psychic damage.

action.name: Spellcasting
entries: The sphinx casts one of the following spells, requiring no spell components and using CHA as the spellcasting ability (spell save DC 17).
At will: detect magic, minor illusion, prestidigitation
3/day each: augury (as an action), major image, suggestion,
tongues
1/day: hallucinatory terrain (as an action)

bonus.name: Psychic Connection
entries: One creature the sphinx can see within 60 feet of it must succeed on a DC 17 WIS save or be mentally connected to the sphinx until the sphinx chooses to end it or until the sphinx dies or the target dies. While connected in this way, the sphinx can cause the target to experience one of the following effects at the start of each of the target’s turns. The effect lasts until the start of the target’s next turn or until the connection ends. The target can repeat the save at the end of each of its turns, ending the connection on a success.
• Disoriented. When the target moves, it moves in a
random direction.
• Distracted. When the target casts a spell, it must
succeed on a DC 17 WIS save or be unable to cast the spell, using the action, bonus action, or reaction to cast the spell but not expending the spell slot or similar limited use of the spell.
• Seeing Double. The target has disadvantage on weapon
attack rolls.

legendary.name: Text
entries: The sphinx can take 3 legendary actions, choosing from the options below. Only one legendary action option can be used at a time and only at the end of another creature’s turn. The sphinx regains spent legendary actions at the start of its turn.

legendary.name: Teleport
entries: The sphinx magically teleports, along with any equipment it is wearing or carrying, up to 120 feet to an unoccupied space it can see.

legendary.name: Cast a Spell (Costs 2 Actions)
entries: The sphinx uses Spellcasting.

legendary.name: Psychic Burst (Costs 2 Actions)
entries: The sphinx sends out a burst of psychic energy. Each creature within 20 feet of the sphinx that has an INT of 8 or higher must succeed on a DC 17 INT save or be incapacitated until the end of its next turn, as it reels from the psychic onslaught. Sporeborn A low hiss emerges from the shambling form crowned with fleshy fungal growth. The sporeborn are humanoid creatures transformed by an infectious fungus, which takes control of victims and uses them as a vehicle to spread its spores into other hosts. The apocalyptic fungus has no real intelligence, only a drive to reproduce. Humanoids can be infected by the fungus through contaminated food or water, breathing the spore clouds, or the bite of an infected host.

legendary.name: Stages of Infection
entries: Creatures infected by the apocalyptic fungus go through different stages of infection before they ultimately expire. Most die before reaching the final stage, though the fungus in their bodies continues to grow, dissolving the host’s remains. If the host body sustains serious damage or can no longer move easily, the fungus attempts to move into a dark, sheltered area so that, as the body decays, the fungus fruits and releases its spores. Sunlight kills the fungus and its spores.

legendary.name: Skullblooms
entries: Infected humanoids that succumb to the fungus become sporeborn skullblooms. Skullblooms show physical signs of their transformation, as the spores inside the host’s brain sprout into spongy stalks that break through the skull to crown the head, while patches of fungus break through the cheeks and around the eye sockets and random areas of the body. Skullblooms stalk or ambush victims and use cooperative tactics, such as herding potential victims toward other skullblooms lying in wait. Sometimes skullblooms enter periods of inertia, lying down or leaning up against a wall or tree and allowing the fungus to spread over them, only to burst out and attack when a potential target passes by.

legendary.name: Bloatblossoms
entries: Skullblooms eventually grow into bloatblossoms. A riot of fungal growth makes the body of these sporeborn broad and ungainly, with layers of tough, fibrous growth that protect it from damage. Bloatblossoms have better echolocation than the younger skullblooms, as well as nodules filled with mycotoxins that they can tear from their bodies and hurl at targets.

monster.name: Skullbloom
languages: understands the languages it knew in life but can’t speak

trait.name: Heightened Hearing
entries: The skullbloom’s Perception is 16 while perceiving by hearing.

trait.name: Plant Resilience
entries: The skullbloom is resistant to exhaustion and to the paralyzed, petrified, and unconscious conditions.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The skullbloom makes one Bite attack and one Slam attack. If the Bite and Slam attacks both hit one Humanoid, the target must succeed on a DC 12 CON save or be infected with apocalyptic fungus (see sidebar). APOCAL YPTIC FUNGUS A disease spread via fungal spores, apocalyptic fungus causes victims to become irritable and sensitive to noise, and in the final stages, fibrous fungal growths sprout from the victim’s skin—particularly around the cranium. Once infected, a Humanoid can’t regain HP except by magical means, and when it finishes a long rest, it must succeed on a DC 13 CON save or suffer two levels of exhaustion. If an infected creature is slain, or if it suffers six levels of exhaustion while infected, it doesn’t die but instead transforms into a skullbloom sporeborn 1 hour after it would have died. Only a wish spell or similarly powerful magic can reverse the transformation.

action.name: Bite
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +3 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 3 (1d4 + 1) piercing damage plus 2 (1d4) poison damage.

action.name: Slam
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +3 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 3 (1d4 + 1) bludgeoning damage.

bonus.name: Instinct to Pursue
entries: The skullboom takes the Dash action toward an uninfected Humanoid it can see or sense.

monster.name: Bloatblossom
senses: keensense 60 ft. (can’t sense beyond this radius)

trait.name: Heightened Hearing
entries: The bloatblossom’s Perception is 16 while perceiving by hearing.

trait.name: Plant Resilience
entries: The bloatblossom is resistant to exhaustion and to the paralyzed, petrified, and unconscious conditions.

action.name: Multiattack
entries: The bloatblossom makes one Bite attack and one Slam attack, or it makes two Toxic Nodule attacks. If the Bite and Slam attacks both hit one Humanoid, the target must succeed on a DC 13 CON save or be infected with apocalyptic fungus (see sidebar).

action.name: Bite
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 6 (1d6 + 3) piercing damage plus 4 (1d8) poison damage.

action.name: Slam
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 6 (1d6 + 3) bludgeoning damage plus 4 (1d8) poison damage. If the target is Medium or smaller, it is grappled (escape DC 13).

action.name: Toxic Nodule
entries: Ranged Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, range 30/60 ft., one target. Hit: 12 (2d8 + 3) poison damage, and the target must succeed on a DC 13 CON save or be poisoned until the end of its next turn.

bonus.name: Instinct to Pursue
entries: The bloatblossom takes the Dash action toward an uninfected Humanoid it can see or sense. Sprite The sound of buzzing wings whines nearby. A tiny humanoid, held aloft on dragonfly wings, appears as though from thin air. Sprites are diminutive fey that protect the deepest forests and the holiest glades. Attentive and well trained in guerrilla tactics, sprites are among the most aggressive and loyal fey. They may appear to travelers and parley, uncovering interlopers’ intentions with their Heart Sight and acting against those whose intentions are suspect.

bonus.name: Unseen Warriors
entries: Sprites travel through the forest in bands of six to one hundred. They mask their numbers using their diminutive size and natural ability to turn invisible. Though sprites have been known to turn would- be trespassers away from their forest homes with traps, noises, or theatrical distractions, they are armed with arrows that can put creatures into a deep sleep. While their quarry sleeps, they may fetch a far more dangerous threat, retreat, or signal for reinforcements to dissuade further hostilit y.

bonus.name: Stern Protectors
entries: Unlike many fey, sprites are serious, even-tempered, and intent on doing as little harm as possible. Those they judge worthy might find themselves protected within the sprites’ territory, while those found wanting could walk headlong into ambushes, traps, and enraged beasts. Sprites refrain from tricks and mischief, and they suffer no evil within their domains.

trait.name: Fey Resilience
entries: The sprite is resistant to the charmed and unconscious conditions.

trait.name: Speak with Beasts and Plants
entries: The sprite can communicate with Beasts and Plants as if they shared a language.

action.name: Shortsword
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 6 (1d6 + 3) piercing damage.

action.name: Shortbow
entries: Ranged Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, range 80/320 ft., one target. Hit: 6 (1d6 + 3) piercing damage, and the target must succeed on a DC 13 CON save or be poisoned for 1 minute. If the save fails by 5 or more, the poisoned target falls unconscious for the same duration, or until it takes damage or another creature takes an action to shake it awake.

action.name: Invisibility
entries: The sprite magically turns invisible until it attacks or casts a spell, or until its concentration ends (as if concentrating on a spell). Any equipment the sprite wears or carries is invisible with it.

bonus.name: Heart Sight
entries: The sprite touches a creature and magically knows the creature’s current emotional state. If the target fails a DC 13 CHA save, the sprite also knows if the creature has malign intentions or intends to commit malevolent acts within the next hour. Celestials, Fiends, and Undead automatically fail the save.

bonus.name: Swift Flight
entries: The sprite moves up to half its speed without provoking opportunity attacks. Star Crow A tiny, pulsing mote weaves through the night. As it approaches, its phosphorescent glow slowly dims into blackness. Seconds later, the thing erupts with a blinding flash. Star crows are tiny, formless alien beings that lack sentience or emotion. These glowing creatures are drawn to sentient beings, feeding ravenously on the beings’ thoughts. They have also been known to carry and transfer memories of one sentient creature to another. Star crows transmit these thoughts as a powerful illusory experience, a pastiche of raw emotion, color, images, and sounds. Some star crows bear the wisdom of primordial beings, while others deliver incomprehensible knowledge of long-dead beings from the Void.

bonus.name: Messengers of the Void
entries: Prophets of the void lords hail star crows as the primordial connection between reality and mind and name them for the messages they exist to deliver. They believe star crows carry ancient scars from elder beings, lost and forgotten memories of dead gods, and tempestuous recountings of a world’s final moments. Deal mongers from the Lower Planes who collect demonic dreams, angelic nightmares, and unspeakable words of power have a great interest in star crows. Cultists prize the forbidden or forgotten knowledge star crows carry.

bonus.name: Immortal
entries: Star crows have an infinite lifespan. They do not age and do not require food, water, air, or rest. The memories each star crow carries degrade, combine, or interlace with the eons of visions a single star crow may possess. A star crow reproduces by splitting, making a perfect copy of itself, when it acquires too many memories to handle without corruption. A newly spawned star crow assumes about a fourth of the information, lessening the burden of its parent.

trait.name: Aberrant Resilience
entries: The star crow is resistant to the charmed, frightened, paralyzed, and stunned conditions, and it has advantage on saves against spells or effects that would alter its form.

trait.name: Illumination
entries: The star crow sheds bright light in a 20-foot radius and dim light for an additional 20 feet.

action.name: Radiant Touch
entries: Melee Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target. Hit: 5 (1d4 + 3) radiant damage, and the star crow attaches to the target. While attached, the star crow can use only the Thought Share action and Blinding Burst bonus action, and it moves with the target whenever the target moves, requiring none of the star crow’s movement. The star crow can detach itself by spending 5 feet of its movement. A creature, including the target, can take its action to detach the star crow by succeeding on a DC 13 STR check.

action.name: Thought Share
entries: The star crow learns some of the attached target’s memories, and the attached target experiences a rapid sequence of memories from other creatures the star crow has encountered. The target takes 5 (2d4) psychic damage and must succeed on a DC 13 CHA save or be stunned until the end of its next turn.

bonus.name: Blinding Burst (Recharge 5–6)
entries: The star crow dims then suddenly bursts with blinding light. Each creature within 30 feet of the star crow must succeed on a DC 13 CON save or be blinded until the end of its next turn. VARIANT: SHADOW STIRGE Stirges nest in dark places, and on occasion, those nests lie in areas where the boundary between the Material Plane and Plane of Shadow is thin. The power of that plane seeps into the nest, infusing the larva with shadow. A murky aura surrounds these stirges, known as shadow stirges.
